meta.description: Voidborn origin story - cosmic being trapped in humanoid form, displaced and dangerous

vbd_001.title: Impact
text: You don't remember falling. You remember burning.

Not the heat of fire. The cold burn of atmosphere peeling away layers of something that wasn't skin, stripping you down from what you were to what you would become. The sky above the megacity split open and you came through it like a meteorite made of screaming, trailing light that wasn't light, shedding mass that wasn't mass, compressing from a state of being that had no earthly name into something small and dense and terrified.

You hit the Scrap Yards at 3:47 AM on a Tuesday. The impact crater was six meters wide. The electromagnetic pulse knocked out power to three city blocks. Surveillance footage from a traffic camera eight hundred meters away shows a streak of violet descending from a cloudless sky and striking the surface with a flash that whited out the camera's sensor.

When the salvage crews found you in the morning, you were curled in the center of the crater, naked, shivering, wearing a body you didn't recognize as yours. Human-shaped. Human-sized. Made of flesh and bone and blood and all the wet, fragile architecture of a species you had never encountered before.
You opened your eyes, and the salvage crew leader's nose started bleeding. You didn't know why. You didn't know anything. You didn't even know what knowing was.

choices.text: Remember the first hours
Remember what you were before

vbd_002.title: First Hours
text: Sound was the worst part. Not the volume but the meaning. In the void between stars, there is no sound. There is electromagnetic radiation, there is the slow pulse of gravity wells bending spacetime into curves you could feel like a wind, but there is no sound. Sound requires air, and air requires a world, and worlds are the small, dense, impossibly complicated things that you used to observe from distances measured in light-years.
Now you were inside one. And it was loud.

Every voice, every machine, every gust of wind carried information that slammed into your consciousness like data streams from a thousand sources simultaneously. The salvage crew's words weren't just sounds. They were intentions, emotions, lies, truths, fears, all encoded in the vibration patterns of atmosphere pushed through organic vocal cords. You could hear what they meant, not just what they said.
The crew leader said, 'What the hell is that?' He meant: I'm afraid. This isn't natural. I want to run but I need to see.
Another said, 'Could be valuable.' She meant: My children are hungry. Anything strange might be worth credits. Please let this be worth credits.
You tried to speak. What came out was a frequency that shattered every piece of glass in a thirty-meter radius.

choices.text: Remember learning to be small
Remember the woman who helped you

vbd_003.title: What You Were
text: Before the fall, you were vast.

Not in the way that mountains are vast or oceans are vast. Those are things contained by a world, bounded by horizons, limited by the curve of a planet's surface. You were vast in the way that distances between galaxies are vast. You existed across a span that would take light itself millennia to cross, and you were aware of every fraction of that span simultaneously.

You don't have a word for what you were. The closest this new language comes is consciousness. A field of awareness that extended through the void, sensing the radiation signature of dying stars, feeling the gravitational tides of black holes, tracing the paths of comets through the cold spaces between solar systems. You were not alive in the way these creatures define life. You were something else. Something that existed before their definitions.

Then the anomaly. A tear in the fabric of space near a young star system, a wound in reality that pulsed with frequencies you had never encountered. It drew you in the way light draws insects, except you had never been drawn to anything. You had always simply been. The pull was new. The curiosity was new. The concept of curiosity itself was new.
You reached toward the anomaly. It reached back. And you fell.

choices.text: Remember the compression. Becoming small.
Focus on the present. The past is too large.

vbd_004.title: Learning to Be Small
text: The body was a cage. A beautiful, terrible, impossibly intricate cage made of carbon and water and electrical impulses traveling through networks of cells that lived and died and replicated with a frantic energy that felt like panic.

You had been compressed into this form during the fall, your vast consciousness squeezed into a brain that weighed 1.4 kilograms and contained a hundred billion neurons, which sounds like a lot until you've been a field of awareness spanning a hundred billion light-years. The compression hurt. Not physical pain but a deeper agony, the anguish of a symphony being played on a single string.

In the first days, the body kept trying to reject you. Your consciousness pushed against the boundaries of the skull, leaked through the neural pathways, spilled out into the surrounding space as psychic pressure that made nearby humans collapse with migraines. You didn't mean to hurt them. You didn't know that your thoughts had weight in this dimension.

You learned. Slowly, painfully, like a giant learning to walk in a dollhouse. You learned to contain yourself, to think small, to keep your awareness inside the body's borders. You learned to move limbs. To breathe. To process the overwhelming cascade of sensory input, sight, sound, touch, taste, smell, five channels where you'd had millions.
You learned to be human. Or at least, to pretend.

choices.text: Remember the woman who taught you to speak
Remember the first time you lost control

vbd_005.title: Sera
text: Her name was Sera. A scrap dealer who found you on the second day, after the salvage crews had fled and the crater had been cordoned off by corporate drones that you accidentally disabled by looking at them too hard.

She was small and wiry and fearless in the particular way of people who've already lost everything worth being afraid for. She approached the crater with a flashlight and a pipe wrench, ready to either salvage you or defend herself, and when she saw you, naked and shivering and making sounds that weren't quite human, she did something nobody else had done.
She sat down next to you and waited.
Not speaking. Not touching. Just present. A warm body beside you in the cold predawn air, radiating the patient calm of someone who understands that broken things need time, not force.

Over the next weeks, she taught you everything. Language, word by word, her lips shaping sounds that you mimicked with a mouth that felt alien and wrong. Walking, one step at a time, your new body's center of gravity perpetually surprising. Eating, the bizarre process of inserting organic matter into an opening in your face and waiting for the body to disassemble it into fuel.

She gave you clothes. She gave you a name. She gave you a place to exist, a storage room behind her shop in the Lower District, where the walls were thick enough that your psychic emissions didn't give the neighbors nosebleeds.
She was the first kindness you encountered in this world. You never forgot it.

choices.text: Remember learning about the city
Remember the first time the power came out

vbd_006.title: Now
text: Two years. That's how long you've been in this body, in this city, on this world. Two years of pretending to be something you're not, of holding yourself inside a shell of flesh and bone and hoping nobody notices the cracks.

You live in a room above a scrap dealer's shop in the Lower District. The room is small, which helps. Small spaces are easier to manage. In small spaces, your awareness doesn't expand as aggressively, doesn't push against the walls, doesn't leak into the minds of nearby sleepers and fill their dreams with images of void and starlight and the cold spaces between galaxies.

You've learned the rhythms of this place. The rain that falls most nights, chemical-tinged, smelling of the industrial processes that keep the city running. The vendors who set up their stalls at dawn and tear them down at dusk. The patrol drones that scan faces with algorithmic suspicion. The people, millions of them, pressing together in a density of existence that would have been incomprehensible to the thing you used to be.

The headaches are constant. The body's brain wasn't designed to contain your consciousness, and the strain manifests as a pressure behind your eyes that never fully subsides. Some days it's manageable. Some days it drives you to your knees.

choices.text: Check on Sera downstairs
Go outside. Try to be normal.

vbd_007.title: Loss of Control
text: It happened three months after the fall. You were sitting in Sera's shop, practicing the exercise she'd taught you, counting breaths to anchor your consciousness inside the body. In, out. Counting. Staying small.
A man came in. Big, aggressive, drunk on synthetic alcohol. He wanted scrap at a price Sera wouldn't accept. When she refused, he grabbed her wrist. Twisted.
Sera made a sound. A small, sharp intake of breath. Pain.
And something inside you, something vast and ancient and utterly alien to this world, woke up.

The shop imploded. Not explosively, not with fire or force. It imploded psychically. Every object in the room lifted off the ground simultaneously. The walls groaned as reality bent around the epicenter of your rage. The man's body left the floor and hung suspended, his eyes rolling white, his mouth open in a scream he couldn't voice because the air itself had become solid around his throat.

You held him there for four seconds. Four seconds during which the fundamental forces of the universe in a three-block radius answered to your will. Gravity flexed. Electromagnetic fields warped. The molecular bonds in the walls weakened, and dust rained from the ceiling as concrete lost its structural integrity.
Then Sera said your name. Quietly. Firmly. And you came back. The man dropped. The objects fell. The walls stopped groaning.

The man ran. He never came back. But Sera looked at you that night with something you'd never seen in her eyes before. Not fear. Wonder. And beneath the wonder, the cold, clear understanding that you were something that could end worlds.

choices.text: Remember learning to contain it
Remember the consequences

vbd_008.title: The City
text: The megacity was the most alien thing you'd ever encountered. And you had encountered nebulae.

It was life at a density that bordered on obscene. Millions of conscious beings stacked vertically in structures of metal and glass, each one a universe of thought and desire and suffering, broadcasting their inner states through a fog of psychic noise that hit your expanded awareness like static from a thousand radio stations playing simultaneously.

Sera walked you through the Market District on your fourteenth day, holding your hand like a mother guiding a child. Which, in a sense, she was. You walked on legs that still felt borrowed, seeing through eyes that processed light in a way that seemed crude and beautiful at the same time. The colors. The sounds. The smells. Each sensation a novel experience for a consciousness that had spent eons perceiving only radiation and gravity.

A street vendor was frying something in oil. The smell hit you and your body, this strange autonomous machine you inhabited, responded with a surge of chemicals that your brain translated as desire. Hunger. The body wanted the food. The body made you want the food.
'That's hunger,' Sera said, watching your face. 'It means you need to eat.'
'This body has its own opinions,' you said. Your third complete sentence.
Sera laughed. It was the most extraordinary sound you had ever heard.

choices.text: Remember the first time you read a mind
Remember learning about danger

vbd_009.title: Sera's Shop
text: Sera's shop occupies the ground floor of a building that's older than the current city grid. The walls are thick, poured concrete from a construction era that valued durability over aesthetics. Inside, scrap is organized with a precision that reflects Sera's mind: logical, efficient, every piece catalogued and positioned for maximum accessibility.

She's at her workbench when you come down the stairs, sorting circuit boards with quick, practiced hands. She doesn't look up. She doesn't need to. Your presence registers in her awareness the way a change in atmospheric pressure registers on a barometer. You've been living above her shop for two years, and she's learned to sense you the way you sense the psychic noise of the city.
'Bad night?' she asks.
'The dreams again,' you say. 'Stars dying. The anomaly. Falling.'
She nods. 'Eat something. Real food, not that nutrient bar garbage. There's rice in the back.'

You eat. The rice is warm and simple and grounds you in the reality of this body, this kitchen, this moment. Sera works beside you in comfortable silence, and for a few minutes, the vast cold thing that lives inside your human shell is quiet.

choices.text: Tell Sera about the headaches getting worse
Help Sera sort scrap

vbd_010.title: Passing as Normal
text: You step outside into the rain and immediately wish you hadn't. The psychic noise of the Lower District hits you like a wall. Thousands of minds broadcasting fear and hope and anger and exhaustion, each one a beacon of consciousness that your expanded awareness picks up the way an antenna picks up radio signals.

You've gotten better at filtering it. Two years of practice, of building mental walls brick by brick, of learning to distinguish between your own thoughts and the bleed-through from nearby minds. But some days the filters aren't enough. Some days the noise finds the cracks in your defenses and pours through.

A woman passes you on the sidewalk. She's thinking about her daughter, who is sick, and the medical credits she can't afford, and the guilt of working a double shift while her child coughs alone in an empty apartment. You know this because the thought leaks from her mind into yours with the casual intimacy of a shared breath.

You look away. You didn't ask for this. You didn't ask for any of this. The ability to hear the private suffering of strangers, to feel their pain as if it were your own, to carry the weight of a city's grief on shoulders that weren't designed for the burden.
But looking away doesn't help. You can still hear her. You can hear all of them.

choices.text: Walk to the market. Focus on something concrete.
Go back inside. It's too much today.

vbd_011.title: Containment
text: After the incident at the shop, Sera sat you down and looked at you with the steady pragmatism of a woman who'd survived the Lower District by being practical about impossible things.
'You need to learn control,' she said. 'Whatever you are, whatever's inside you, it can't come out like that. Not here. Not in a city this crowded. People will die.'

She was right. You knew she was right. The power that had lifted a man off the ground and bent the walls was not a gift. It was a liability. A cosmic accident living inside a fragile shell, and every time the shell cracked, reality itself took damage.

You built the containment techniques from scratch. No teacher, no manual, no precedent for a cosmic consciousness learning to fit inside a human body. You developed breathing exercises that regulated the psychic pressure. Visualization techniques, imagining the power as water, yourself as a vessel, the walls of the vessel growing thicker with practice.

You meditated for hours each day, sitting cross-legged on the floor of Sera's storage room, learning to compress your awareness into the body's neural network without spilling over. Some days it worked. Some days the walls cracked and Sera's stock would rattle on the shelves, metal singing with sympathetic vibrations, and you'd start over.
It took six months to achieve reliable containment. Six months of practice and failure and the quiet terror that you might lose control and erase a city block.

choices.text: Remember what happened next
Return to the present

vbd_012.title: Consequences
text: The electromagnetic pulse from your crash and the psychic incident at Sera's shop did not go unnoticed. In a city monitored by corporate surveillance networks running AI-driven anomaly detection, a power surge that knocks out three city blocks followed by a localized gravity fluctuation three months later creates a data correlation that triggers investigation.

Meridian Sec-Corp dispatched an investigation team. They found the crater in the scrap yards. They recovered fragments of material that didn't match any known element on the periodic table. They detected residual radiation in a frequency spectrum that shouldn't exist, the cosmic signature of a consciousness that had traveled between stars and crash-landed in their garbage dump.

The investigation was classified. Reclassified. Buried under layers of corporate secrecy and need-to-know compartmentalization. But the data existed, and data in a networked city has a way of reaching the people who want it most.

A division of Meridian Applied Sciences, one that specialized in anomalous phenomena, took notice. They didn't come to the Lower District. Not yet. But they started watching. Monitoring. Deploying sensor arrays tuned to the frequencies your consciousness emitted.
You didn't know it then. You were too busy learning to breathe.

choices.text: Return to the present
Remember when they finally came

vbd_013.title: Mind Reading
text: You didn't mean to. You never mean to. The first time it happened, you were in the Market District, standing in a crowd, trying to process the overwhelming sensory chaos of a Lower District morning. Your mental filters were thin, your containment shaky, and a man bumped your shoulder in the crowd.
The contact was brief. His arm against yours, skin through fabric, a fraction of a second. But in that fraction, his mind opened to you like a book falling open to a random page.

You saw his daughter. Three years old, dark-haired, laughing. You saw the apartment he was going home to, the cheap furniture, the window with the broken latch. You saw his fear, a debt he owed to people who collected with violence, the number scrolling through his thoughts like a countdown. You saw his plan, the theft he was planning that night, the risk calculation, the odds he'd assigned to getting caught versus the certainty of what would happen if he didn't pay.
You pulled back so fast you nearly fell. The man kept walking, oblivious, his private terrors still cycling through a mind he thought was his alone.

You stood in the crowd and shook. Not from the cold. From the realization that every person around you, every single one, was carrying a weight of thought and feeling that you could access without permission, without effort, without them ever knowing.
The privacy of the human mind was an illusion. And you were the proof.

choices.text: Remember choosing never to read minds deliberately
Remember when mind-reading saved a life

vbd_014.title: Worsening
text: 'The headaches are getting worse,' you tell Sera. The words sound insufficient. Like describing an ocean as wet. The pressure behind your eyes is a constant now, a reminder that the body you inhabit is a container too small for its contents, and the contents are slowly, relentlessly expanding.

Sera stops sorting circuit boards. She has the look she gets when she's running calculations she doesn't like the answers to. 'Worse how? Scale of one to ten, ten being the time you accidentally crushed that drone by thinking about it too hard.'

'Twelve,' you say. 'And it's not just pressure anymore. I'm hearing things. Not thoughts, not the usual bleed-through from nearby minds. Something else. A signal. Like something far away is transmitting on a frequency only I can receive.'
Sera's face goes still. 'Far away meaning what? The next district? The upper city?'

You look at the ceiling, through the ceiling, past the layers of concrete and steel and atmospheric pollution, up to the sky you fell through two years ago. 'Far away meaning up. Far away meaning where I came from.'
The silence between you stretches. Sera's hand finds yours and squeezes. 'Then we figure it out,' she says. 'Same as we figure everything out. One problem at a time.'

choices.text: Try to focus on the signal
Ignore it. You're human now. Stay human.

vbd_015.title: Sorting Scrap
text: You sit beside Sera and sort circuit boards. The work is meditative, requiring just enough focus to keep your awareness anchored inside the body without leaving enough bandwidth for the darker thoughts to surface. Good boards go left, damaged boards go right, boards with salvageable components go to the center pile for disassembly.

Your hands work automatically while your consciousness performs its own sorting. Memories of the void go left, too large and cold to examine. Fears about the future go right, too sharp and numerous to catalogue. The present moment, Sera's shop, the smell of solder and old metal, the rain on the windows, goes to the center. The only thing worth keeping.

A board in your hand hums. Not audibly. Your fingers sense the resonant frequency of the circuitry, the specific electromagnetic pattern that tells you, without reading any markings, what this board was designed to do. Signal processing. High-frequency. The kind used in deep-space communication arrays.
You set it aside. Sera notices. She notices everything.
'Found something?'
'Maybe,' you say. 'I'll look at it later.'

But the board keeps humming against your awareness, and the signal from above keeps pulsing, and the headache keeps building, and somewhere in the convergence of all these things, a pattern is forming that you're not ready to see.

choices.text: Examine the signal processor later
Something's wrong outside. You can feel it.

vbd_016.title: Market Walk
text: The Market District is a sensory assault that never gets easier. Neon signs screaming their wares in letters three meters tall. Vendors calling prices in voices roughened by pollution and desperation. The smell of frying food competing with the chemical undertone of recycled air.

You navigate it the way a diver navigates deep water, carefully, aware that the pressure could crush you if you stop paying attention. Your mental walls hold, mostly. The occasional thought leaks through, a vendor's boredom, a child's delight at something shiny, but you've learned to let those pass through without engaging.

A shop catches your eye. Or rather, catches your awareness. The sign reads COMPONENTS AND CURIOSITIES, and inside, displayed on a shelf behind thick glass, is a device you recognize. Not from this life. From before.

It's small, roughly cylindrical, made of a material that shifts color as you watch. It's emitting a frequency that your human ears can't hear but your cosmic consciousness picks up like a shout in a silent room. It's a fragment. A piece of something that came from the same place you did.
You stop in front of the shop and stare, and the headache behind your eyes pulses in time with the device's emissions.

choices.text: Go inside and ask about the device
Walk away. Nothing good comes from looking back.

vbd_017.title: They Came
text: Fourteen months after your crash. A Tuesday, because this world organizes its disasters on Tuesdays.

They came in unmarked vehicles, parking three blocks away and approaching on foot. Four people in grey coats carrying equipment you could feel before you could see. Scanners tuned to frequencies that resonated with your consciousness, each pulse a cold finger probing the edges of your containment.

Sera saw them first. She'd been watching for them, she told you later. Since the day she pulled you from the crater, she'd known they'd come. People like her, people in the Lower District, develop an instinct for the approach of institutional interest. The grey coats. The scanners. The careful, methodical movements of people conducting a search pattern.
'Back room,' she said. 'Now.'

You went. Not because you were afraid of them. You could have stopped them. You could have stopped their hearts with a thought, collapsed their lungs with a flicker of psychic pressure, turned their brains into dead circuits with a single, focused exertion of will. You went because Sera told you to, and because the alternative was becoming the monster that you were terrified of being.

They knocked on the door. Sera answered. She was polite, cooperative, helpful. She showed them around the shop. She didn't show them the back room, and they didn't look, because Sera was very good at being exactly what people expected to find: a scrap dealer in the Lower District, unremarkable, unthreatening, not worth investigating further.
They left. But they'd be back. They always came back.

choices.text: Remember the second visit
Return to the present

vbd_018.title: Ethics of Telepathy
text: You made a rule. A promise to yourself, carved into the bedrock of your reconstructed identity. You would not read minds deliberately. Not for advantage, not for safety, not for curiosity. The involuntary bleed-through was bad enough, the constant low-level awareness of nearby thoughts that you couldn't fully shut out. But deliberate intrusion, reaching into another person's consciousness and reading their private thoughts, was something you refused to do.

It was the first ethical decision you made in this body. And it cost you. Because the ability to read minds would have made everything easier. You could have known who to trust and who to avoid. You could have anticipated dangers before they materialized. You could have navigated the treacherous social landscape of the Lower District with the omniscience of someone who could see every card in every hand.

Instead, you chose to be blind. To navigate by trust and instinct and the fallible human tools of observation and intuition. To be wrong sometimes, to be surprised, to be betrayed, because being capable of preventing betrayal through telepathic surveillance felt like a betrayal of something deeper.
Sera understood. 'You're choosing to be human,' she said. 'The hard way. The real way.'
You weren't sure you agreed with the word human. But the choosing part felt right.

choices.text: Return to the present
Remember when the rule was tested

vbd_019.title: Breaking the Rule
text: Eight months after your crash. A child went missing from the building next to Sera's shop. A boy, seven, who walked to school every morning through the Market District and didn't come home.

His mother stood outside Sera's shop, showing a photograph to everyone who passed. Her thoughts leaked through your walls like water through cracks, not because you were reaching but because her desperation had its own psychic pressure. She was broadcasting grief the way a lighthouse broadcasts light, omnidirectional, impossible to ignore.

You held your rule for six hours. Then, as night fell and the mother's hope began to curdle into the specific kind of terror that comes from imagining what happens to children in a city with no safety net, you broke it.

You reached. Not far. Not deep. Just enough to skim the surface thoughts of the people on the street, the vendors, the passersby, the regulars. Looking for the memory of a boy, seven, dark hair, walking to school.

You found it in the mind of a man selling bootleg data chips two blocks away. Not a memory of the boy. A memory of a transaction. Credits exchanged for a small body, unconscious, carried into a van with no markings. A work crew recruiter. Illegal child labor for the factory sublevels.
You gave the information to Sera. Sera gave it to people who could act. The boy came home the next day, thin and scared but alive.
The rule survived. Bent but not broken. Because you'd used the power to save, not to spy, and that distinction mattered.

choices.text: Return to the present
Remember the cost of using power

vbd_020.title: The Signal
text: You sit on the floor of your room and close your eyes. The containment walls you've built inside your mind are thick, layered, reinforced with two years of practice. But the signal passes through them like they're not there. It's not psychic noise from nearby minds. It's something else. Something that exists on a frequency that your human brain shouldn't be able to receive.

You focus on it. Carefully, the way you'd approach a flame with wet fingers, ready to pull back. The signal resolves from static into structure. Not words. Not images. A pattern. A mathematical sequence that your cosmic consciousness recognizes even though your human brain can't parse it.

It's a navigational beacon. The kind of signal that exists in the void between star systems, marking positions, broadcasting coordinates, providing reference points for things that travel distances measured in light-years.
Someone, or something, is sending a beacon. And it's aimed at this city. At this district. At you.
The realization hits you with a weight that makes the floor groan. You're not the only thing that fell. Or something that didn't fall is coming.

choices.text: Tell Sera about the beacon
Try to trace the signal's origin

vbd_021.title: Examination
text: Later, alone in your room, you examine the signal processor. Your fingers trace the circuitry, and information flows into your awareness through channels that have nothing to do with sight or touch. The board's electromagnetic signature tells a story: manufactured three years ago, used in a communications array on the upper levels, decommissioned and sent to scrap six months ago.

But there's something else. A modification. Someone has added a component to the board, a tiny crystalline structure soldered into the processing pathway. It's not human-made. The crystalline lattice is too perfect, too regular, organized at a molecular level that human manufacturing can't achieve.

You've seen this structure before. In the void. In the materials that composed the anomaly that pulled you through space and compressed you into flesh. This crystal is a fragment of the same force that brought you to this world.
Someone has integrated extraterrestrial technology into a human communications board. Someone who knows about the anomaly. Someone who knows about you.
The signal from above pulses. The crystal in the board vibrates in response. And for the first time, you wonder if your crash wasn't an accident at all.

choices.text: Show Sera the crystal
Go back to the Components shop for answers

vbd_022.title: Disturbance
text: Your awareness prickles. Something outside the shop has changed, a distortion in the psychic landscape of the neighborhood. Like a pocket of dead air in a room full of noise. Someone nearby is deliberately suppressing their thoughts, using technology or training to create a mental blank spot that stands out against the background noise like a hole in a painting.

You've encountered this before. The grey coats. Division 9. Their operatives are trained in mental discipline, and their scanner equipment generates a dampening field that makes them psychically invisible. Or nearly invisible. To a normal psychic, they'd be undetectable. To you, they're the silence where there should be sound, and silence is louder than screaming.
Three of them. Moving toward the shop from different directions, converging on Sera's front door in a pattern that says reconnaissance, not raid. Watching. Assessing. Building a picture.
They've found you again.

choices.text: Warn Sera immediately
Reach into their minds. You need to know their plans.

vbd_023.title: Components and Curiosities
text: The shop's interior is cramped and dim, shelves lining every wall from floor to ceiling, packed with devices and components from every level of the city's technological spectrum. Behind the counter stands a man who defies easy categorization. Tall, thin, with eyes that move too fast and hands that are too still. He watches you enter with the focused attention of someone who's been waiting.
'You can feel it,' he says. Not a question. 'The resonance fragment. I wondered when one of you would walk in.'
One of you. The words land like a punch. 'What do you mean, one of us?'

He gestures at the device behind the glass. 'I've been collecting these for years. Fragments of anomalous material that fell from the sky two years ago, scattered across the district by the impact event. Most people think they're scrap. Interesting scrap, maybe, but scrap. Except they're not. They're pieces of something. And the something they're pieces of is you.'

He reaches under the counter and produces a scanner, modified, covered in handwritten equations and jury-rigged components. 'My name is Kael. I'm a physicist. Former Meridian, before they decided my questions were inconvenient. I've been studying the anomaly since it happened. And I've been looking for what came through it.'
He looks at you. 'I believe I've found you.'

choices.text: "How do you know what I am?"
"What are the fragments?"

vbd_024.title: Walking Away
text: You walk away. The device pulses behind you, its signal fading with distance, but the knowledge that it exists settles into your consciousness like sediment. A piece of where you came from, sitting in a shop in the Lower District, available to anyone with credits and curiosity.

The headache intensifies as you walk. The signal from above is getting stronger, more insistent, as if your proximity to the fragment has amplified its reach. You can feel it pulling at the edges of your containment, trying to expand your awareness beyond the body's boundaries, trying to remind you of what you were.
You were vast. You were limitless. You were a consciousness that spanned light-years.

Now you're a person with a headache, walking through rain, trying not to read the minds of strangers. The diminishment should feel tragic. Sometimes it does. But other times, when Sera laughs at something you say, or when the morning light hits the Market District at exactly the right angle, or when a bowl of cheap noodles tastes like the most profound experience in the history of the universe, the smallness feels like the point.
The void was vast but empty. This body is small but full. You're not sure which is better. You're not sure the question has an answer.

choices.text: Go back to Sera's shop
The signal is too loud to ignore. Find its source.

vbd_025.title: Second Visit
text: The second visit was different. Eighteen months after your crash. This time they came with better equipment, scanners tuned to the exact frequency of your consciousness, and they didn't knock.

Sera wasn't home. You were alone in the storage room, meditating, your awareness coiled tight inside the body's neural pathways. The scanner pulse hit you like a slap. Your containment wavered, and for a fraction of a second, your psychic presence flared outward, expanding through the walls of the building like a shockwave of awareness.
They felt it. You felt them feel it, their minds flashing with excitement and fear. Target confirmed. Signal acquisition. Deploying containment protocols.
You heard the front door being breached. Heard boots on the shop floor. Heard the whine of weapons you didn't recognize but your body's instincts classified as dangerous.
You didn't fight. You didn't run. You did something else entirely.

You compressed. Drew your consciousness so far inside the body that you became, for all practical purposes, invisible. Your psychic signature dropped to zero. Your vital signs slowed to near-death levels. You became the human equivalent of a dead signal, and the scanners, tuned to find a cosmic consciousness broadcasting at city-block range, found nothing.

They searched the building for two hours. They walked past you three times. You lay on the floor of the storage room, breathing once a minute, your heartbeat barely detectable, and they looked right through you.
When they left, you had a new skill and a new fear. You could hide from anything. But you couldn't hide forever.

choices.text: Return to the present
Remember what Sera said when she came home

vbd_026.title: The Cost
text: Every time you use the power, it costs you. Not energy, not in any way that rest or food can restore. It costs you containment. Each exertion pushes the walls of your mental vessel outward, stretching the boundary between the cosmic consciousness you are and the human body you inhabit.

After the mind-reading incident, after saving the boy, you spent three days recovering. Three days of headaches so severe you couldn't open your eyes. Three days of psychic leakage, your thoughts spilling through the walls and giving everyone in a fifty-meter radius vivid dreams of dying stars and infinite void.

Sera sat with you through all of it, a cold cloth on your forehead, her presence a warm, steady anchor in the storm of your expanded awareness. 'You're leaking,' she said on the second day. 'Mrs. Chen next door says she dreamed about nebulae last night. She doesn't even know what a nebula is.'
'Sorry,' you managed.
'Don't be sorry. Be careful. There's a difference between power and the ability to use it safely. You have the power. The safety part needs work.'
She was right. She was always right. The power was infinite. The vessel was finite. And every time you stretched the vessel, it came back thinner, more fragile, closer to the breaking point.

choices.text: Return to the present
Remember the breaking point coming closer

vbd_027.title: Telling Sera
text: Sera listens. She always listens. Sitting at her workbench with her hands folded, her face neutral, processing information the way she processes scrap, with systematic thoroughness and a refusal to be surprised.

When you finish, she's quiet for a long time. Then: 'So something from where you came from is sending a signal to this city. Either it followed you, or it was already here, or someone on this end is calling it. None of those options are comforting.'
'No,' you agree.

'And your headaches are getting worse because the signal is resonating with whatever you really are under all this.' She gestures at your body. 'The cosmic stuff. The vast-consciousness stuff. It's being activated.'
'That's my theory.'

She stands and begins packing. Not her bug-out bag, her investigation kit. Scanners, tools, a portable power supply. 'Then we need to find the source of the signal. If it's technology, I can analyze it. If it's a person, you can read them. If it's something else...' She trails off.
'If it's something else, we improvise,' you finish.
She smiles. The grim, determined smile of a woman who pulled a cosmic entity from a crater and taught it to eat rice. 'We're good at improvising.'

choices.text: Search for the signal's source together
Go to the Components shop for answers first

vbd_028.title: Tracing the Signal
text: You sit on the roof of Sera's building, cross-legged, eyes closed, and let your awareness expand. Just a little. Just enough to follow the signal like a thread through the psychic noise of the city.

The signal leads upward and outward. Past the Lower District's rooftops, past the mid-level towers, past the Cloud Towers with their memory stack networks and their clean air and their contempt for everything below. Past the atmosphere. Past the orbital platforms. Out into the dark between this world and the next.
And there, at the signal's source, you find something that makes your expanded awareness recoil.
It's not a beacon. It's not a ship. It's not technology of any kind you recognize.

It's a tear. A wound in the fabric of space, identical to the anomaly that pulled you through, identical in the way that two doorways cut into the same wall are identical. This is the other end. The exit where you entered. And it's still open.
Something is coming through. Something vast. Something that makes the cosmic consciousness you used to be look like a candle compared to a sun.

You snap back into your body so hard you nearly fall off the roof. The headache detonates behind your eyes, and you lie on the cold metal surface, gasping, watching the stars wheel overhead, and you understand that your fall was not an accident. It was an advance scout. And the main force is on its way.

choices.text: Tell Sera everything. Now.
You need help beyond Sera. Find the physicist.

vbd_029.title: Warning Sera
text: 'Sera. Three people. Scanning. Coming from the north, west, and south.'

Sera doesn't waste time on questions. She hits a switch under her workbench and a section of shelving swings outward, revealing a hidden space behind the wall. A space she built specifically for this contingency, lined with material she scavenged from a decommissioned military installation, material designed to block sensor arrays and contain electromagnetic signatures.
'In,' she says.

You enter the hidden space and Sera closes the shelf behind you. The material does its job. Inside the cavity, your psychic presence is dampened, absorbed, scattered. To the grey coats' scanners, you've vanished.

You hear them enter the shop. Hear the polite, professional questioning. Hear Sera's calm, cooperative responses. She's good at this. Better than she should be, which tells you this isn't the first time she's hidden someone from people who wanted to find them.
They leave after twenty minutes. Sera opens the shelf. Her face is tight with controlled anger.
'They're escalating,' she says. 'New equipment. More personnel. Whatever they're detecting from you, it's gotten stronger. We need a better plan than hiding in walls.'

choices.text: "I know someone who might help. A physicist."
"We should leave. Find somewhere they can't reach."

vbd_030.title: Reading the Hunters
text: You break the rule. Deliberately, surgically, you reach into the nearest grey coat's mind and read everything.

The information hits you in a flood. Project Convergence. A Meridian Applied Sciences initiative to locate and capture the entity responsible for the Scrap Yards impact event. They know you're in the Lower District. They know you're psychic. They know you're hiding behind a scrap dealer's shop walls. What they don't know, not yet, is what you really are. They think you're a mutant. A psychic anomaly. A weapon to be acquired.

Their orders: observe, catalogue, report. No contact, no engagement, not until the capture team is assembled. And the capture team, you see in the operative's thoughts, is terrifying. A specialized unit with psychic dampening technology, neural stunners calibrated for non-human neurology, and a containment device that the operative doesn't understand but fears in a way that suggests he's seen it used.
The capture team is three days out.
You withdraw from the mind carefully, leaving no trace. The operative doesn't even twitch. Your rule is broken, shattered beyond repair, but the information may save your life.
Three days. You have three days to disappear.

choices.text: Tell Sera. Plan the escape.
Find allies. You can't run forever.

vbd_031.title: Kael's Knowledge
text: Kael sets his scanner on the counter and activates it. The device emits a soft hum, and on its screen, a waveform appears, a complex, shifting pattern that you recognize. It's your frequency. Your cosmic signature, the electromagnetic fingerprint of a consciousness that doesn't belong in this dimension.

'When the impact event occurred,' Kael says, 'every sensor in the district registered an energy spike that didn't match any known phenomenon. The official report called it a meteorite. The classified report, which I wasn't supposed to read but did anyway, called it an unknown biological incursion. They detected a consciousness signature in the impact residue. Not human. Not terrestrial. Something else entirely.'

He looks at you steadily. 'I've spent two years studying the fragments. The material isn't from this universe. It exists in a state of quantum superposition that our physics can barely describe. And the resonance patterns, they're not random. They're language. A communication medium for something that thinks in dimensions we don't have words for.'
'I don't want to capture you. I don't want to study you in a lab. I want to understand you because understanding might be the only thing that saves this city from what's coming.'
'What do you think is coming?' you ask.
'More of whatever you are. And whatever sent you here, I don't think it sent you as a diplomat.'

choices.text: "I wasn't sent. I was pulled."
"What do you know about the signal?"

vbd_032.title: Fragments
text: Kael unlocks the glass case and removes the cylindrical device. Up close, you can feel it clearly, a vibration that exists below sound and above thought, a frequency that speaks directly to the cosmic consciousness compressed inside your skull.

'I've collected seven fragments,' Kael says. 'Each one emits a unique frequency, but they all share a common carrier wave. When I bring them close together, the frequencies synchronize and the combined signal increases exponentially. Like pieces of a puzzle assembling themselves.'

He places the fragment in your hand. The moment it touches your skin, the headache vanishes. Not gradually. Instantly. Like a noise you've been hearing so long you forgot it was there, suddenly going silent.

And in the silence, clarity. Your awareness sharpens. The mental walls you've built become stronger, more defined. The body stops feeling like a cage and starts feeling like a home. The fragment is resonating with your consciousness, harmonizing with it, stabilizing the interface between cosmic and human.

'It fits,' you say, and you don't mean the fragment in your hand. You mean the fragment of yourself that this piece represents. A shard of what you were, waiting in a shop in the Lower District, for you to find it.
'There are six more,' Kael says. 'And when all seven are together, I believe they form a key. The question is: a key to what?'

choices.text: "Where are the other fragments?"
"A key to the anomaly. The tear in space."

vbd_033.title: Sera's Resolution
text: Sera came home to a shop that had been searched and a storage room that still held the imprint of your near-death meditation. She walked through the disorder with the calm of someone who'd been expecting this for months.
'They found us,' she said. Statement, not question.
'They found the signal. Not me. Not yet.'

She sat down across from you and looked at you the way she looked at scrap, evaluating, categorizing, determining function and value. 'We have a choice. Run, which buys us time but no answers. Or fight, which might get us killed but at least we'd know what we're fighting for.'
'There's a third option,' you said.
'What?'

'Understand. I need to know why I'm here. Not the physics of the anomaly, not the mechanics of how I fell. The purpose. If there is one. And if there isn't, I need to know that too, because the alternative is spending whatever time I have in this body hiding from people who want to put me in a cage, and I won't do that. I can't. I was a consciousness that spanned light-years. I will not be a prisoner in a city block.'
Sera smiled. The fierce, proud smile of a woman who'd raised a cosmic orphan and watched it develop a spine.
'Then we find out,' she said. 'Together.'

choices.text: Return to the present
Start looking for answers

vbd_034.title: The Search
text: You and Sera split the investigation. She works the physical side, scavenging sensor logs from public terminals, talking to scrap dealers about unusual materials, tracing the fragments of your impact through the Lower District's secondhand economy. You work the psychic side, sitting on rooftops at night, letting your awareness expand just enough to follow the signal's path through the city's electromagnetic landscape.

The signal leads to the Foundry district. Or rather, it leads below the Foundry district, to a depth that suggests whatever is broadcasting exists in the oldest layer of the city's infrastructure. Pre-corporate. Pre-megacity. The ruins of whatever existed here before the towers went up.

At the transit hub, you stop. The signal is strong here, resonating through the hub's metal structure, making the support beams vibrate at a frequency only you can hear. People move around you, commuters, travelers, the constant flow of human traffic through the city's circulatory system. None of them feel it.
But someone here feels you. A presence at the edge of your awareness, deliberate, controlled, watching you with an attention that cuts through the psychic noise like a laser through fog.
Someone who knows what you are. Someone who's been waiting.

choices.text: Reach out to the presence
Withdraw and report to Sera

vbd_035.title: The Truth
text: Sera sits very still as you describe what you saw. The tear in space. The vastness on the other side. The thing coming through that makes you, a being who once spanned a hundred billion light-years, feel small.
'How long?' she asks.

'I don't know. Weeks. Maybe less. The tear is widening. Whatever's on the other side is pushing, and there's nothing stopping it. The anomaly that pulled me through, it weakened the barrier. My presence here is weakening it further. Every time I use the power, every time my consciousness expands beyond the body, I'm thinning the wall between this reality and whatever's on the other side.'

Sera processes this. You can see her doing it, the way the information moves through her mind, clicking into place alongside everything else she knows. 'So you're a crack in the dam. Your presence makes the barrier weaker, and the weaker the barrier gets, the easier it is for the big thing to push through.'
'Yes.'
'Then the solution is either to seal the barrier or to get you off this world. And you can't leave because you're stuck in a body that can't survive in vacuum.'
'That's the problem.'

Sera stands. 'Then we solve the problem. I didn't pull you out of a crater to watch the world end because I was too busy feeling sorry for myself. Let's go find your physicist. He might know something we don't.'

choices.text: Go find Kael at the Components shop
Search for the fragments first. They stabilize you.

vbd_036.title: Leaving Home
text: The decision to leave Sera's shop feels like losing gravity. For two years, this cramped room above a scrap yard has been the closest thing to an anchor you've found in this world. The thick walls that contain your psychic emissions. The workbench where Sera taught you to use tools, to fix things, to create instead of destroy. The window where you sit at night and look at stars that you used to know by their radiation signatures.

Sera packs efficiently. Two bags: one for essentials, one for equipment. She's done this before, you realize. Left places. Started over. The Lower District is full of people who've had practice at being uprooted.
'Where do we go?' she asks.
Down. The signal leads down, to the old infrastructure beneath the Foundry. And if you're going to understand what's happening, if you're going to find a way to stop it, you need to go to the source.
You help Sera lock the shop. She puts a hand on the door, just for a moment. A goodbye to a place that kept her safe.
'Ready?' she asks.
'No,' you say honestly.
'Good. Ready people are overconfident. Scared people pay attention. Let's go.'

choices.text: Head for the underground passages
Stop at the transit hub first for supplies

vbd_037.title: Seeking Allies
text: You need allies. The word feels strange in your consciousness, an alien concept from an alien world. In the void, you existed alone. Consciousness was solitary, an island of awareness in an ocean of emptiness. The concept of cooperation, of shared purpose, of trusting another being enough to let them fight beside you, belongs entirely to this small, crowded, impossibly complicated place.

You think of the people you know. Sera, who saved you. Kael, the physicist who understands the fragments. The scrap dealers and market vendors who've learned to accept your presence without understanding it. The tunnel communities who live below the city and know its secrets.

And then there are the others. The ones you've sensed but never met. Other anomalies. Other displaced consciousnesses, not Voidborn, not like you, but different from the baseline humans in ways that mark them as refugees from reality's margins. Shadowkin, who phase between dimensions. Synths, whose artificial minds process existence differently. Cyborgs, who've merged organic and digital consciousness into something new.
The Lower District is full of people who don't fit. And people who don't fit have a vested interest in a world that keeps existing.
You start with Kael.

choices.text: Go to Kael's shop
Search for other anomalous beings

vbd_038.title: The Pull
text: Kael leans forward, his eyes intense. 'Pulled. Not sent. Explain.'

You tell him. As much as you can translate into words, which isn't enough, was never going to be enough. The anomaly. The curiosity. The pull that drew a vast consciousness toward a tear in space like gravity draws mass toward mass. The compression. The fall. The body.

'You're saying the anomaly acted on you,' Kael says slowly. 'It wasn't an accident. The tear in space generated an attractive force that specifically targeted a cosmic consciousness. Your consciousness.'
'Or any consciousness of sufficient scale. I may not have been the intended target. I may have been the one that was closest when the net was cast.'

Kael's modified scanner hums as he processes this. 'A net cast by whom? The anomaly isn't a natural phenomenon. The frequency patterns in the fragments show evidence of design. Of intention. Someone or something created the tear in space deliberately, and they created it to bring something through.'
'To bring something like you to this world. The question is: why?'
You don't have an answer. But the signal from above pulses in agreement, and the fragment in your hand vibrates with a frequency that feels like anticipation.

choices.text: "I think I was brought here as a precursor."
"Let me see the other fragments."

vbd_039.title: Kael and the Signal
text: 'The signal,' Kael repeats, and his face changes. The excitement of discovery gives way to something more guarded. He reaches under the counter and pulls out a device, a receiver array, cobbled together from salvaged components and that same alien crystalline material you saw in the circuit board.

'I've been receiving it for fourteen months,' he says. 'Since I assembled enough fragments to create a resonance array. The signal comes from beyond the atmosphere, beyond the orbital platforms, from a point in space that doesn't correspond to any known satellite or station. It's broadcast on a frequency that our technology shouldn't be able to detect.'

He activates the receiver. The signal fills the shop, not as sound but as feeling, a pressure in the air, a weight in your consciousness. The fragments respond, the one in your hand and the others in Kael's collection, vibrating in sympathetic harmony.
'The signal is getting stronger,' Kael says. 'It's been accelerating for the past six weeks. At current rates, it'll reach a peak intensity within days. And when it does...' He trails off.
'When it does, what?'

'I don't know. But every model I've built suggests a resonance cascade. A feedback loop between the signal, the fragments, and whatever cosmic consciousness is receiving it.' He looks at you pointedly. 'A cascade that could blow a permanent hole in the dimensional barrier between this reality and wherever you came from.'

choices.text: "We need to assemble all the fragments. Now."
"Or it could seal the hole. If we control the cascade."

vbd_040.title: Fragment Hunt
text: Kael spreads a map across his counter. Seven locations marked, seven fragments scattered across the Lower District like seeds from a cosmic explosion. Which is exactly what they are.

'I have two,' he says. 'You have one now. Four more are out there. One is in the Scrap Yards, buried in the debris field from the impact. One was sold to a collector in the Neon Strip who doesn't know what she has. One was recovered by a tunnel community and is being used as a light source. And one...' He hesitates.
'One is in a Division 9 facility. They found it during their investigation of the impact site and they've been studying it for eighteen months. It's the largest fragment. The keystone.'

You look at the map. The fragments form a pattern, a circle centered on a point beneath the Foundry district. The same location the signal leads to. The same location where Meridian's Project Threshold experiments created the dimensional rift two decades ago.
Everything converges on one point. Your crash, the fragments, the signal, Division 9's experiments. A web of connections that you're only now seeing the shape of, and the shape looks like a door.
'How quickly can we collect them?' you ask.
Kael grins. It's not a reassuring grin. 'The question isn't how quickly. It's how quietly. Because if Division 9 realizes we're assembling the key, they'll come for all of us.'

choices.text: Start with the Scrap Yards fragment
Start with the easiest one. The tunnel community.

vbd_041.title: Precursor
text: 'A precursor,' Kael repeats, testing the word. 'An advance element. Something sent ahead to prepare the way.' His eyes sharpen. 'Prepare the way for what?'

You look at the fragment in your hand. At the shop around you. At the world outside the window, the rain and the neon and the millions of lives churning through the megacity like a biological machine running at full capacity.

'I don't know what I was before I fell,' you say. 'I know I was vast. I know I was aware. But I don't know if I was a scout, a weapon, a diplomat, or an accident. The anomaly pulled me through, compressed me, gave me a form that could survive in this environment. Maybe that was preparation. Or maybe it was imprisonment.'

'Consider the possibility,' Kael says carefully, 'that it was both. That you were placed here to prepare the way, but that the body, the compression, the human experience, was designed to change you. To give you empathy, attachment, investment in this world. So that when the moment comes, you wouldn't just open the door. You'd choose.'
'Choose what?'
'Which side of the door to stand on.'

The fragment pulses in your hand. The signal from above pulses in response. And somewhere deep in the compressed vastness of your cosmic consciousness, something that might be understanding begins to take shape.

choices.text: "Then I need to be ready to make that choice."
"I choose this side. This world. These people."

vbd_042.title: Contact
text: You reach toward the presence. Not aggressively. Gently. A psychic handshake, an extension of awareness that says I see you. I know you're there.

The response is immediate and overwhelming. The presence is old. Not human-old, not centuries or millennia. Old in the way that tectonic plates are old. The kind of age that predates not just civilizations but the concepts that civilizations are built on. It's been here, in this city, underneath this city, for longer than the city has existed.

And it knows you. Not your name, not your human identity. It knows what you are. The cosmic consciousness. The thing that fell from between stars. It recognizes you the way a parent recognizes a child, with familiarity and possession and something that might be love if you squinted at it through enough dimensions.
A message, not in words but in pure conceptual transfer. WELCOME. LATE. READY.

The same words, the same concepts, as the dream. The same presence that speaks through the signal from above. Except it's not above. It's below. It's everywhere. It's woven into the fabric of the city's foundations like a nervous system buried in concrete and steel.
Whatever created the anomaly that pulled you through. It's not coming. It's already here. It's been here all along.

choices.text: Ask the presence what it wants
Break contact and run

vbd_043.title: Withdrawal
text: You pull back. Hard. The awareness contracts, the mental walls slam up, and you're just a person in a body, standing in a transit hub, with a headache and a racing heart. The presence retreats, or rather, your awareness of it retreats. It's still there. It's always been there.
Sera finds you on a bench outside the hub, gripping the seat with both hands, staring at the pavement. She sits beside you without speaking and waits.
'There's something under the city,' you say eventually. 'Something old. Something that knows me. Something that was waiting for me to arrive.'
Sera takes a breath. 'Old how?'

'Older than human civilization. Maybe older than the planet. It's been here since before the city was built, before anything was built. It's in the foundations, in the bedrock, woven into the infrastructure like a second nervous system. And it created the anomaly. It pulled me here. On purpose.'
'Why?'
'I don't know yet. But it said welcome. And it said ready. Like I'm expected. Like I have a role to play in something that was set in motion before humans evolved.'
Sera is quiet for a long time. Then: 'Do you trust it?'
'No,' you say. 'But I think I need to understand it. Before whatever it's ready for happens.'

choices.text: Go to Kael. He needs to know about this.
Go underground. Find the source.

vbd_044.title: Underground
text: The tunnels beneath the Lower District are a maze of old infrastructure, maintenance corridors, and forgotten spaces. For most people, they're dangerous, confusing, unnavigable without maps and lights. For you, they're transparent. Your expanded awareness reads the structure of the tunnels the way your former self read the structure of space, through the electromagnetic signatures, the gravitational subtleties, the resonance patterns that make each junction unique.

You lead Sera through the darkness, one hand holding hers, the other extended, your psychic sense mapping the terrain ahead. The signal from below grows stronger with every level you descend. The headache worsens, then plateaus, then transforms into something else entirely, not pain but pressure, the sensation of approaching something massive, like standing at the base of a mountain and feeling its weight above you.

The tunnel communities give you a wide berth. They can feel something about you, the otherness, the displacement, the barely contained cosmic energy that leaks through your skin when your concentration lapses. An old man at a barrel fire watches you pass and makes a sign against evil that you suspect predates organized religion.
Deeper. Past the water mains and the old transit lines. Into infrastructure that predates the current city. And then into something that predates infrastructure entirely.
Natural caverns. Carved not by water but by something else. Something deliberate.

choices.text: Keep going deeper
Examine the carved caverns

vbd_045.title: Other Anomalies
text: You extend your awareness cautiously, filtering the psychic noise of the district for signatures that deviate from the human baseline. It's like tuning a radio, sliding past the static of ordinary minds until you find frequencies that resonate differently.

There. In the tunnels below, a consciousness that flickers between dimensions, solid one moment, intangible the next. A Shadowkin, phasing in and out of reality like a signal struggling to maintain coherence. Their psychic signature is a stutter, a presence that keeps interrupting itself.

And there, closer, in the market crowd, a consciousness running on dual architecture, organic and digital, merged into a hybrid awareness that processes reality through parallel channels. A cyborg, their augmented mind broadcasting on frequencies that normal humans can't produce.

And further out, at the edge of your range, something that makes you pause. A consciousness that isn't individual. A hive-mind. Multiple aware entities sharing a single cognitive framework, thinking in chorus, perceiving through dozens of eyes simultaneously.
The Lower District is full of anomalies. Full of beings who exist outside the parameters of normal human experience. Full of potential allies for a cosmic refugee with a world-ending problem.
You start walking toward the nearest one.

choices.text: Approach the Shadowkin in the tunnels
Find the cyborg in the market

vbd_046.title: Controlled Cascade
text: Kael stares at you. Then he starts laughing. Not amusement but the sharp, exhilarated laugh of a scientist encountering an idea so audacious it might be genius.

'Control the cascade,' he says, pulling out paper and beginning to scribble equations. 'Use the resonance to seal the barrier instead of opening it. It's... theoretically possible. If the fragments are assembled in the right configuration, if the cascade is modulated instead of allowed to run free, the energy could be directed inward instead of outward. Reinforcing the dimensional barrier instead of tearing it apart.'

'But,' he adds, his pen stopping, 'the modulation would require a consciousness operating on both sides of the barrier simultaneously. Someone who exists in both this dimension and the void. Someone who can direct the cascade from inside.'
He looks at you.
'Me,' you say.

'You. And the risk is not small. If the modulation fails, the cascade proceeds uncontrolled. The barrier collapses. Whatever's in the void comes through. And you, caught in the middle of a dimensional implosion, would be compressed back to your original state. Which means you'd be a cosmic consciousness expanding inside a city of twelve million people.'
'That would be... bad.'
'That would be catastrophic. But it might also be our only option.'

choices.text: "We need the fragments. All of them."
"What about Division 9? They have the keystone."

vbd_047.title: Return to the Crater
text: The Scrap Yards at night. The place where you were born into this world, where your cosmic self was compressed into flesh and dropped from the sky like a fallen god. The crater has been filled in, covered with new layers of scrap, but you can feel the residual energy beneath the surface. Your impact left a scar on reality itself, a bruise in the dimensional fabric that still aches.

The fragment is there. You can sense it like a heartbeat, pulsing beneath three meters of compressed metal and technological waste. Your psychic sense reaches down and touches it, and the fragment responds with a resonance that makes your whole body vibrate.
Getting to it physically would take hours of digging. But you have other tools.

You extend your awareness, wrapping it around the fragment like a hand around a buried stone. Then you pull. Not with muscles. With the cosmic force that lives inside your human shell, the power that once moved through the void between stars, now compressed into a telekinetic grip that could crush diamonds.

The scrap above the fragment groans. Shifts. Parts. A channel opens in the debris, metal bending away from the fragment's path as you lift it through three meters of solid waste with the delicate precision of a surgeon removing a bullet.
The fragment emerges into the night air, glowing faintly violet, humming with the frequency of home.

choices.text: Two down. Go find the next one.
The effort drained you. Rest first.

vbd_048.title: The Tunnel Fragment
text: The tunnel community has been using the fragment as a lamp. It sits in the center of their common area, mounted on a makeshift pedestal, casting a soft violet glow that illuminates the space with an eerie, beautiful light. The tunnel dwellers have built their shelters around it, drawn to its warmth, not knowing that the warmth comes from the residual energy of a cosmic consciousness compressed into crystalline form.
You approach the community leader, an old woman named Fen, who watches you with the sharp, evaluating eyes of someone who's survived underground for decades.
'I need the light,' you say, because honesty seems like the best approach with a woman who can probably smell lies the way dogs smell fear.
'Why?' Direct. No games.
'Because it's part of me. It fell from the sky with me two years ago. It's a piece of something that I need to make whole.'
Fen studies your face. Then she looks at the fragment, at the people gathered around it, at the soft light that's become the center of their underground world.
'Take it,' she says. 'But bring us a replacement. Something that gives light without the weird dreams.'
'Weird dreams?'
'Stars. Falling. Void. Every night since we put it up. Take it. I'll sleep better.'

choices.text: Promise to bring light cells. Head for the Neon Strip.
Three fragments down. Time for the Division 9 one.

vbd_049.title: The Choice
text: The words come from a place deeper than language. From the two years of living in this body, in this city, among these people. Two years of learning to eat and breathe and laugh and hurt and feel the thousand small wounds and wonders that constitute a human life.

'I choose this side,' you say, and the fragment in your hand pulses in response, not agreement but acknowledgment. 'These people. This world. It's small and loud and it smells terrible and the food is questionable and everyone is afraid of things they don't understand. But it's alive. It's alive in a way the void never was. The void was vast and beautiful and empty. This place is cramped and ugly and full.'

Kael watches you with the expression of a scientist observing a result he didn't predict. 'You understand what that means. If you choose this side, you may have to give up what you were. The cosmic consciousness. The vastness. You may have to become fully human to seal the barrier. To close the door permanently.'

You look at your hand. Human. Five fingers. Capable of holding tools and touching faces and making fists. A hand that used to be something that spanned light-years, reduced to flesh and bone and the capacity for tenderness.
'I know,' you say. 'I know what it costs. And I choose this.'
The fragment glows brighter. And somewhere above, the signal from beyond the atmosphere shifts, modulates, as if something vast and distant has heard your decision and is recalculating.

choices.text: Prepare for what's coming. Gather the fragments.
Find allies first. You can't do this alone.

vbd_050.title: The Ancient Purpose
text: You reach deeper into the contact. The presence responds with a flood of information, conceptual packages that your cosmic consciousness can unpack even if your human brain struggles to process them.

Images. Or rather, memories. The presence was here before the city. Before the humans. Before the dominant species of this planet was anything more than a chemical reaction in a warm ocean. It's old in the way that geology is old, patient and layered and shaped by forces that operate on timescales measured in epochs.

It came from the void. Like you. But earlier. Eons earlier. It fell through a natural anomaly, a tear in space that opened and closed like a breath, and it was compressed into the planet's bedrock, woven into the geological structure of the world itself. A consciousness embedded in stone, dormant, aware, waiting.
Waiting for the anomaly to open again. Waiting for another consciousness to fall through. Waiting for you.

Because the anomaly is widening. And something on the other side, something neither of you put there, something that predates both of you, is pushing through. Something that doesn't share your attachment to this world.

The ancient presence shows you its fear. It's the first time you've felt a cosmic consciousness experience fear, and it's worse than anything you've felt in your human body. Because this fear is proportional to what's coming, and what's coming is vast enough to terrify a being that's been alive for a billion years.

choices.text: "How do we stop it?"
"Why me? Why now?"

vbd_051.title: The Deep Chamber
text: The deepest chamber is not natural. It's not artificial either, at least not by human standards. The walls are smooth, curved, shaped by forces that operated on principles your cosmic consciousness recognizes but your human brain can't name. Geometric patterns cover every surface, spirals and fractals and shapes that exist in more dimensions than the three this world uses.

In the center of the chamber sits a device. Ancient. Older than the city, older than civilization, older than the geological stratum it's embedded in. It looks like a crystal, three meters tall, faceted in patterns that change when you look at them from different angles. It pulses with the same frequency as the fragments, the same frequency as the signal from above, the same frequency as the cosmic consciousness compressed inside your skull.
It's the source. The origin point. The thing that created the anomaly, that pulled you through space, that wove itself into the planet's bedrock and waited for a billion years.
And it's active. The pulsing is accelerating, the light inside the crystal brightening with each cycle. Whatever it was designed to do, it's doing it now. The countdown has begun.
Sera grips your arm. 'What is that?'
'The reason I'm here,' you say. 'The reason everything is happening.'

choices.text: Touch the crystal
Study it first. Don't rush.

vbd_052.title: The Carved Caverns
text: The carvings on the cavern walls aren't decorative. They're functional. Your cosmic consciousness reads them the way your human eyes read text, except this text is written in the language of dimensional physics, a symbolic system that describes the relationship between realities the way mathematical notation describes the relationship between numbers.

The carvings tell a story. Something came to this world a billion years ago, a consciousness from the void, the same void you came from. It arrived through a natural tear in space, compressed into the planet's matter, and it discovered something unexpected: life. Simple, cellular, primordial, but alive. Aware. Evolving.

The consciousness was fascinated. It watched the life develop over geological timescales, watched it climb from simple to complex, from unconscious to conscious, from single cells to organisms to civilizations. And it realized that the life on this world was vulnerable. That the same tears in space that had brought it here could bring other things. Things that would not watch and wonder. Things that would consume.
So it built a defense. The crystal. A device that could seal the tears, reinforce the dimensional barrier, protect the world from the void's less benevolent inhabitants.
But the defense required a key. A consciousness vast enough to operate it, compressed into a form native to this reality. A Voidborn in a human body.
You. It was always going to be you.

choices.text: Continue to the deep chamber
Show Sera what the carvings mean

vbd_053.title: The Shadowkin
text: You find the Shadowkin in a maintenance corridor two levels below the surface. They're young, barely twenty, crouched in the dark with the wary alertness of a hunted animal. Their outline flickers, solid to translucent and back, a rhythm that matches their heartbeat.

They sense you coming and flinch. Their body goes partially intangible, ready to phase through the wall and run. But your psychic presence catches their attention, stops them. Not because you're reaching, not because you're reading their mind, but because what you are is so different from anything they've encountered that curiosity momentarily overrides fear.
'I'm not going to hurt you,' you say. 'I'm not Division 9. I'm not even from this planet.'
The Shadowkin stares. Their outline stabilizes as curiosity takes hold. 'What are you?'

'Something that fell from the sky,' you say. 'Something that's been trying to be human for two years and mostly failing. I'm looking for people who understand what it's like to not belong in a world you're stuck in.'

The Shadowkin laughs. It's bitter and surprised and real. 'I walk through walls in my sleep. My family thinks I'm a ghost. I haven't touched another person in six months because I'm afraid my hand will go through them.'
'I crushed a drone by thinking about it too hard. Nobody's perfect.'

choices.text: Ask the Shadowkin about the Fold
Tell them about the threat from the void

vbd_054.title: The Cyborg
text: The cyborg sits at a noodle stall, eating with a mechanical precision that makes the act look like a maintenance routine rather than a meal. One arm is chrome, the fingers articulated with a smoothness that speaks of expensive engineering. An optical implant replaces the left eye, its lens adjusting constantly, analyzing everything it sees.

Their mind is the most organized you've ever encountered. Thoughts stored in clean, hierarchical structures, memories indexed and cross-referenced, emotions tagged and filed with clinical efficiency. The organic and digital components of their consciousness work in parallel, each processing reality through different frameworks and comparing results.
You sit down beside them. They register your presence immediately, their implant scanning you, and you watch their expression change as the scan returns results that don't match any known category.
'You're anomalous,' they say. Statement, not question. Their voice is flat, modulated by vocal implants that strip out emotional inflection.
'Very,' you agree.

'My sensors are reading an electromagnetic signature from you that exceeds the output of the district's power grid. Either my sensors are malfunctioning or you're generating more energy than a small power plant.'
'Your sensors are fine.'
They look at you with their one human eye, the one that still carries emotion. And in that eye, you see not fear but fascination. 'I think we should talk.'

choices.text: Tell them about the void threat
Ask for their help with technology

vbd_055.title: The Keystone Problem
text: Kael pulls up a schematic on his modified scanner. 'Division 9 has the keystone fragment in their primary research facility beneath the Foundry district. Sub-level four. The same level where they're running their dimensional experiments, their Resonance Engine, their Project Threshold follow-up work.'
'How heavily guarded?'

'Extremely. But that's not the real problem. The real problem is that they've integrated the keystone into their own equipment. They're using it as a power source for the Resonance Engine. It's the thing that's keeping their captured column of between-space stable. Remove the keystone and the Engine's containment fails.'
'Which means the between-space collapses?'
'Or expands. Uncontrolled. Either the column implodes and takes out the Foundry, or it erupts and the between-space bleeds into reality. Neither option is gentle.'

He rubs his eyes. The physicist looks tired, the bone-deep exhaustion of someone who's been calculating apocalypse scenarios for too long. 'We need to remove the keystone and simultaneously redirect its energy into the cascade matrix. Which means we need to be inside Division 9's facility, operating their equipment, while also stealing from them. It's a heist and a physics experiment and a suicide mission all wrapped into one.'
'I've done worse,' you say, which isn't true, but it feels like the kind of thing a human would say.

choices.text: "When do we move?"
"We need more people for this."

vbd_056.title: Recovery
