text: The telekinetic extraction costs you. The headache returns with compound interest, a pressure behind your eyes that makes the world pulse and blur. You sit in the alley behind the scrap yards, holding the two fragments you've collected, and wait for the pain to subside.

The fragments help. Their resonance stabilizes your consciousness, eases the compression, makes the body feel less like a cage and more like clothing that fits. With each fragment you collect, the containment improves. Your control sharpens. The power becomes less dangerous not because it diminishes but because you can direct it more precisely.

Sera finds you in the alley, guided by the violet glow of the fragments. She sits beside you and puts a hand on your shoulder. The contact is grounding, a physical anchor to this reality, this moment, this body.
'Two down,' she says. 'How do you feel?'
'Like I'm remembering how to be myself. Not the void self. Not the human self. Something between. Something new.'
She squeezes your shoulder. 'Something new is good. The world could use something new.'
You rest. And for the first time in two years, you dream not of falling but of rising.

choices.text: Continue the fragment hunt
Check in with Kael about the cascade plan

vbd_057.title: The Neon Strip
text: The Neon Strip is the Lower District's beating heart of commerce and vice. The name is literal, a kilometer-long stretch of shops, bars, and entertainment venues, each one trying to out-illuminate the others with competing displays of neon, LED, and holographic projection. The result is a sensory overload that makes the Market District look like a monastery.

The collector Kael mentioned runs a curiosity shop near the Strip's center. The sign reads ANOMALOUS ACQUISITIONS in letters that shift color every three seconds. Inside, the shelves are lined with objects that defy easy explanation: devices from unknown manufacturers, materials with unusual properties, artifacts that hum or glow or vibrate at frequencies that set your teeth on edge.

The collector herself is a small woman with augmented eyes that whirr as they refocus, recording everything they see. She looks at you and her eyes spin faster, processing data that clearly doesn't match her expectations.
'You're the impact event,' she says. 'Or part of it. I've been tracking the fragments since the crash. Most dealers think they're industrial waste. I know better.'

She reaches under the counter and produces the fragment. It glows in her hand, violet and warm. 'I'll sell it to you. But not for credits. I want information. I want to know what you are and where you came from and why the sky opened up and dropped you on my city.'
Fair enough.

choices.text: Tell her the truth
Give her a partial truth. Protect yourself.

vbd_058.title: The Defense
text: The ancient presence responds with a plan. Not words, not images, but a structural understanding that downloads directly into your consciousness like software being installed.

The crystal in the deep chamber is a dimensional anchor. When activated by a Voidborn consciousness operating through the assembled fragments, it generates a field that reinforces the barrier between realities. A seal. A permanent one. The tears in space that allow passage between the void and this dimension would be closed, cauterized, healed.

But there's a cost. The anchor requires a consciousness to power it. A vast consciousness, cosmic-scale, compressed and channeled through the crystal's architecture. The ancient presence itself is too deeply embedded in the planet to disengage. It needs a partner. A younger consciousness, recently fallen, still flexible enough to interface with the crystal without being absorbed.
You.

The cost is this: to seal the barrier, you would need to channel your entire consciousness through the crystal. Not just the compressed version living in your human body. All of it. The vastness you were before the fall, the awareness that spanned light-years. You would need to remember what you were and become it again, briefly, terrifyingly, and then pour all of that cosmic power into a seal that would lock the barrier forever.
And afterward, you would be empty. Human. Truly human. The cosmic consciousness burned away, spent like fuel, leaving behind just the body and the two years of memories you've accumulated in it.
The choice is clear. Godhood or humanity. The void or the world.

choices.text: Accept the plan. Begin preparing.
"There has to be another way."

vbd_059.title: Why You
text: The ancient presence answers with memories older than continents. It has pulled others through the void over the eons. Others like you, vast consciousnesses compressed into the bodies of whatever dominant species inhabited the world at the time. Each one was a candidate. Each one was given the same choice: seal the barrier or let the void consume the world.
Some chose to seal it. Their sacrifices bought centuries of safety, millennia, before the barrier degraded and new tears appeared and new candidates had to be found.
Some refused. Those worlds didn't survive.

You're the latest in a line that stretches back billions of years. Not the first, not the last, just the one who's here now, in this moment, when the barrier is weakest and the threat from the void is strongest. You were pulled through because you were close. Because you were curious. Because curiosity, in a cosmic consciousness, indicates the capacity for attachment, and attachment to a world is the fuel that powers the seal.
You fell because you were capable of choosing to stay.

The irony is not lost on you. A being that spanned light-years, pulled from the void by its own curiosity, given a body and a name and a bowl of rice, and asked to give up everything it was to protect a world it's only known for two years.
The universe has a brutal sense of humor.

choices.text: "How do I prepare?"
Return to Sera with this knowledge

vbd_060.title: Crystal Contact
text: You touch the crystal. The moment your skin makes contact, the world explodes into meaning.

Every sensation you've experienced in two years of human existence, every taste, every touch, every moment of joy and pain and confusion, rushes through you simultaneously. Not as memory but as data, processed by the crystal's architecture, translated from human experience into cosmic understanding.

The crystal is reading you. Learning you. Cataloguing every aspect of the consciousness that lives inside this human body, mapping the interface between cosmic and mortal, measuring the strength of the bonds that hold you to this world.

It finds Sera. The warmth of her hand. The sound of her laugh. The patience of her teaching. It finds the rice. The rain. The neon glow of the Market District at night. It finds the child you saved. The minds you read. The power you chose not to use.

It finds love. Not romantic love, not specific, not directed at one person. A broader, stranger, more diffuse love. The love of a cosmic consciousness for a world that is small and temporary and beautiful in a way that the void, with all its vastness, could never be.

The crystal accepts you. The pulsing steadies. The light brightens. And inside your mind, a doorway opens to a place between what you are and what you were, and in that doorway stands the choice you'll have to make.

choices.text: Accept the purpose
Tell Sera what you've learned

vbd_061.title: Study First
text: You circle the crystal, studying it with both human eyes and cosmic awareness. The surface patterns shift as you move, revealing new configurations, new mathematical relationships. It's a language, you realize. The same language as the carvings on the cavern walls. The same language as the fragments' resonance patterns. A communication system designed for beings that think in dimensions.

Sera takes readings with her scavenged scanner equipment. The numbers she reports are impossible by the standards of human physics, energy outputs that exceed the crystal's apparent mass by orders of magnitude, radiation in spectra that shouldn't exist, gravitational anomalies that suggest the crystal is denser than the surrounding bedrock despite being visibly crystalline.

'This thing is old,' Sera says, understating magnificently. 'And it's not made of anything I recognize. The molecular structure is...' She shakes her head. 'It's not molecular. It's something else. Something that uses atoms the way we use bricks, as building blocks for a structure that exists at a level beneath the atomic.'
'It's a dimensional anchor,' you say. 'A device that reinforces the barrier between realities. And it needs me to activate it.'
Sera looks at you. 'What does activation cost?'

You know the answer. You've known it since you first felt the signal. But saying it out loud, to the woman who taught you to eat and breathe and be human, is the hardest thing you've done in any form of existence.

choices.text: Tell Sera the truth about the cost
"I don't know yet. I need to learn more."

vbd_062.title: Sharing Knowledge
text: You translate the carvings for Sera, word by painstaking word, converting dimensional physics into something a human mind can hold. She listens with the focused intensity she brings to everything, her engineer's brain filing each piece of information into a structural framework.

'So you're a key,' she says when you finish. 'A cosmic consciousness, compressed into a human body, designed to interface with an ancient defense system that protects this world from things in the void. And the system needs all seven fragments to function, and one of them is in the hands of people who want to use you as a lab rat.'
'That's the summary, yes.'
'And the cost of activating this defense is your... vastness. The cosmic part. You'd lose it. Become fully human.'
'That's the cost.'

Sera is quiet. She looks at the carvings, at the ancient story of a consciousness that loved a world enough to protect it, and you can see her doing the emotional math. Weighing the cosmic against the personal. The vastness of what you'd lose against the smallness of what you'd keep.
'Is being human so bad?' she asks softly.
You think about rice. About rain. About the sound of her laugh. 'No,' you say. 'It's not bad at all.'

choices.text: Continue to the crystal chamber
Go back up. Gather the remaining fragments.

vbd_063.title: The Fold Connection
text: The Shadowkin's name is Dex. They know about the Fold. They've been avoiding it.
'Lira's people,' Dex says, their outline flickering with nervous energy. 'They want to recruit me. I've been saying no. I don't do groups. Groups get noticed, and noticed gets you disappeared.'
'Division 9 is going to find you whether you're in a group or alone,' you say. 'At least in a group, you have people watching your back.'
'You sound like Lira.'
'I've never met Lira. But I'm a cosmic consciousness trapped in a human body and even I can see the value of not being alone.'
Dex stares at you for a long moment. Then: 'You really aren't from here, are you?'

'I'm from between stars. And something worse than me is on its way. The dimensional barrier that your phasing keeps weakening? It's the only thing between this world and the void. And the void is pushing.'
The Shadowkin's flickering outline goes very still. 'Well,' they say. 'That's terrifying.'
'Yes. But terrifying problems have a way of making former loners reconsider their stance on teamwork.'
Dex looks at you. Looks at the tunnel around them. Looks at the future they can feel closing in. 'Take me to Lira,' they say.

choices.text: Find the Fold together
Gather more allies first

vbd_064.title: The Warning
text: You tell them about the void. About what's coming. About the tear in space, the ancient presence beneath the city, the fragments, the crystal, the choice. You tell them everything because you've decided that the time for secrets is over. Whatever's coming will not be stopped by one person, one consciousness, one anything. It will be stopped by everyone, or it won't be stopped at all.

Your audience, whether Shadowkin or cyborg or scrap dealer or physicist, listens with the specific silence of people hearing something they wish they could unhear. The truth about the void is not comfortable. It's not reassuring. It's the kind of truth that rewrites your understanding of reality and leaves you standing in the wreckage of your previous worldview, looking for something to hold on to.
When you finish, the silence stretches. Then someone speaks.
'What do you need from us?'

The question comes from a different person depending on who's listening, but it always comes. Because the people of the Lower District, the ones who've been forgotten and discarded and ground down by a city that treats them as expendable, understand one thing with crystalline clarity: when the world is ending, the only thing that matters is what you do about it.

'I need you to help me get the keystone fragment from Division 9,' you say. 'I need you to help me reach the crystal beneath the Foundry. And I need you to be ready for what happens when I activate the seal.'
'What happens?'
'Honestly? I don't know. But I'd rather face it together than alone.'

choices.text: Plan the Division 9 infiltration
Gather the remaining fragments first

vbd_065.title: Technical Alliance
text: The cyborg's name is Vessel. Not their birth name, the name they chose when they decided that the distinction between organic and artificial was less important than function. They're an engineer, a hacker, a systems architect who understands technology at a level that borders on the intuitive.

'Your energy signature is extraordinary,' Vessel says, their augmented eye spinning as it processes data. 'If I could interface with it, channel it through a properly configured array, we could do things that this city's technology wasn't designed for.'
'Such as?'

'Such as penetrating Division 9's security network. Their systems run on electromagnetic shielding and quantum encryption. Your output could overwhelm both. You'd be a skeleton key for every locked door in the district.'

The idea is appealing and terrifying in equal measure. Using your power deliberately, channeling it through technology, extending it beyond the body's limits. It's the opposite of containment. It's weaponization.
'I need to be careful,' you say. 'Every time I use the power, it weakens the dimensional barrier.'
Vessel considers this. 'Then we use it precisely. Surgical strikes. Maximum impact, minimum expenditure. I build the framework, you provide the energy. We get in, get what we need, get out.'
'Efficient.'
'I'm a cyborg. Efficiency is religion.'

choices.text: Work with Vessel to plan the infiltration
Introduce Vessel to Kael

vbd_066.title: The Infiltration Plan
text: The planning session happens in a bar in Sector 7, a dive called The Rust Bucket where the clientele minds their own business because minding your own business is the price of admission. You sit in a back booth with your assembled team, whoever that includes, and spread Kael's maps and schematics across the table.

Division 9's facility beneath the Foundry is a fortress. Multiple security levels, biometric access, phase-dampening fields, electromagnetic shielding, and armed personnel. Getting in through conventional means is impossible.

But you're not conventional. You're a cosmic consciousness with telekinetic abilities and a team of anomalous beings who, between them, can phase through walls, hack any system, and sense hostile minds before they get within range.

'Three entry points,' you say, pointing at the schematic. 'The ventilation system, the waste processing tunnel, and through the wall. I can feel the keystone from here. It's on sub-level four, in the main lab, integrated into the Resonance Engine. We need to reach it, disconnect it from their equipment without causing a cascade, and get it out.'
'And if we can't disconnect it safely?' Sera asks.
'Then I contain the cascade. I hold the between-space in check with my consciousness while you get the keystone clear. It's risky. If I lose control, the containment collapses.'
'And if containment collapses?'
'Then we have bigger problems than one missing fragment.'

choices.text: Execute the plan
Do one more reconnaissance first

vbd_067.title: Recruitment Drive
text: You spend two days building a team. Each recruitment is different, each potential ally requiring a different approach, a different truth, a different answer to the question that everybody asks: why should I risk my life for a cosmic refugee and a physics experiment?

The answers vary. For Sera, it's loyalty. For Kael, it's knowledge. For the tunnel communities, it's the promise that someone will fight for them when nobody else will. For the Shadowkin, it's solidarity among outcasts. For the cyborgs and augmented humans, it's the engineer's compulsion to fix broken systems.

By the end of the second day, you have a team. Small, unlikely, held together by shared purpose and the kind of trust that forms between people who know they're probably going to die but have decided that dying for something is better than surviving for nothing.

Sera looks at the assembled group. A scrap dealer, a physicist, a Shadowkin, a cyborg, and a cosmic entity in a human body. 'We're the least likely resistance movement in the history of the species,' she says.
'We're the only one this species has got,' you reply.
She smiles. The fierce one. The one that means let's go.

choices.text: Begin the mission
Finalize the plan

vbd_068.title: Full Truth
text: You tell her. Everything. The void. The fall. The compression. The body. The power. The ancient presence beneath the city. The signal from above. The thing that's coming through the tear in space.

The collector listens without interrupting. Her augmented eyes record every word, every micro-expression, every subtle shift in your electromagnetic output. When you finish, she's quiet for thirty seconds, an eternity in conversation, while her eyes process the data.
'I believe you,' she says. 'Not because your story is credible. It's insane. I believe you because my sensors are reading your energy output and the numbers don't lie. You are not from this world.'
She hands you the fragment. 'Take it. No charge. Information isn't payment for something like this. It's a privilege.'
She leans forward. 'But I want in. Whatever you're planning, whatever you're building, I want to be part of it. I've spent my life collecting anomalies. I've never been part of one.'

Her name, she tells you, is Nyx. And she has connections throughout the Lower District's underground economy. Contacts, safe houses, supply lines. The infrastructure of someone who's been preparing for something extraordinary without knowing what shape it would take.

choices.text: Welcome Nyx to the team
Go collect the remaining fragments

vbd_069.title: Partial Truth
text: 'The fragments are pieces of a device,' you say, which is true as far as it goes. 'A device that can stabilize the dimensional anomalies that have been increasing in the Lower District. Phase events. Reality fluctuations. The things that Division 9 has been investigating and making worse.'

The collector's augmented eyes narrow. She can tell you're holding back. People with that kind of technology can usually detect the gaps in a statement, the places where truth bends around something unspoken.
'And the device needs all seven fragments to function,' she says.
'Yes.'
'And you need it to function because...?'

'Because if it doesn't, something very bad happens. Something that affects everyone in this city, not just the people in the Lower District. Something that the corporate towers won't be able to buy their way out of.'
She considers this. Then she hands you the fragment. 'I'll sell it for three hundred credits. Fair market value for anomalous material. I'm a businesswoman, not a charity.'
You pay. It cleans out the last of your reserves, but the fragment settles into your hand with a weight that feels like progress.

choices.text: Four down. Go for the tunnel fragment.
Time to face the Division 9 problem.

vbd_070.title: Preparation
text: You prepare. Not with weapons or equipment, though those are gathered too. You prepare the way a Voidborn prepares: by expanding your awareness, by strengthening the interface between cosmic and human, by learning to hold both states simultaneously without either one destroying the other.

The fragments help. Each one you've collected adds stability, clarity, control. With four fragments in your possession, the headaches are gone. The containment is effortless. The power flows through you like current through a well-designed circuit, available but directed, potent but safe.

You practice channeling. Directing the cosmic energy through the fragments, shaping it into specific frequencies, targeting specific effects. Telekinesis refined from a blunt instrument into a scalpel. Psychic sensitivity tuned from a flood to a stream. The vast power of a cosmic consciousness, filtered through the human experience of two years, becoming something new. Something that is neither god nor mortal but a bridge between the two.
Sera watches you practice and her eyes hold something you haven't seen before. Pride. The pride of a woman who pulled something alien from a crater and raised it into something worth being.
'You're ready,' she says.
'I'm terrified,' you say.
'Those aren't mutually exclusive. In my experience, the best people are always both.'

choices.text: It's time. Begin the mission.
One last night. Savor the human moments.

vbd_071.title: Another Way
text: The ancient presence considers your objection with the patience of something that has existed for a billion years and has heard every argument the universe has to offer.
Then it shows you the alternatives. Three paths, laid out with the crystalline clarity of a consciousness that operates in dimensions you can barely perceive.
Path one: seal the barrier and lose the vastness. Become human. Live a human life. Die a human death.

Path two: don't seal the barrier. The void breaks through. Everything on this world ends. Not quickly. Slowly. The between-space consuming reality centimeter by centimeter, like frost spreading across a window, until nothing remains but the cold and the dark and the things that live there.

Path three: find another Voidborn. Another cosmic consciousness compressed into mortal form. Share the burden of the seal between two beings instead of one. The cost halved but the difficulty doubled, because finding another Voidborn in a universe this large is like finding a specific grain of sand on a beach that spans galaxies.
Except you already know there are other anomalies. Other beings displaced from their natural state. The Shadowkin phase between dimensions. Who's to say one of them isn't more than they appear?
The ancient presence pulses with what might be surprise. It hadn't considered that.

choices.text: Search for another Voidborn among the anomalies
Accept that the sacrifice may be necessary

vbd_072.title: Sera's Response
text: You tell Sera about the cost. About losing the vastness. About becoming truly, irrevocably human. About the choice between godhood and mortality, between the void and the world.
Sera listens. And then she does something you don't expect. She cries.

Not dramatically. Not with sobs or wailing. Just tears, sliding quietly down a face that has weathered the Lower District's worst without breaking. Tears for you. For the thing you might have to give up. For the cosmic consciousness that learned to eat rice and laugh at bad jokes and sit quietly in a scrap dealer's shop while rain hammered the windows.

'I'm not crying because I'm sad,' she says, wiping her face with the back of her hand. 'I'm crying because I'm proud. Because two years ago, something vast and alien fell from the sky, and it could have been anything. It could have been a weapon, a disaster, an extinction event. But instead it was curious. And kind. And willing to give up everything it was to protect a world it barely knows.'

She takes your hand. 'Whatever you decide, I'm with you. If you seal the barrier and lose the vastness, I'll be here. You'll still have the rice and the rain and a room above a scrap shop. You'll still have me. And that has to be worth something.'
'It's worth everything,' you say. And you mean it with every cell of the body you never asked for but have learned to call home.

choices.text: Prepare for the final mission
Gather the team. It's time.

vbd_073.title: Meeting the Fold
text: Dex leads you to the Fold's sanctuary through passages that shift and blur as the Shadowkin phases through walls you have to walk around. The deep tunnels are cold and dark and alive with the resonance of the ancient presence buried in the bedrock.

The sanctuary opens before you. Shadowkin move through the space, their outlines flickering, their bodies phasing in and out of visibility with the casual ease of people who've accepted their impossible nature. The mural on the far wall glows with luminescent paint, figures emerging from shadow, reaching for each other across darkness.

Lira meets you at the entrance. She's shorter than you expected, wiry, with tattoos that move like living things across her arms. Her left hand is transparent, glass-clear, the bones visible inside. She looks at you with eyes that have seen Division 9's worst and survived it.
'Dex says you're from space,' she says flatly.
'That's the simplified version.'
'Give me the complicated version. I've had a complicated day.'
You tell her. About the void, the fragments, the crystal, the seal, the thing that's coming. Lira listens with the stillness of a predator evaluating whether something is food or threat.
'So the dimensional barrier that our phasing weakens,' she says, 'is the same barrier you want to seal. And sealing it might stop the phasing. Might make us... normal.'
The implication hangs between you. Helping you might cost the Shadowkin their defining trait.
'Or it might stabilize you,' you say. 'Controlled phasing instead of involuntary. You'd still be Shadowkin. Just safer ones.'

choices.text: Ask for Lira's help with Division 9
Share what you know about the Resonance Engine

vbd_074.title: The Brain Trust
text: Kael and Vessel meet in the Components shop. The physicist and the cyborg circle each other for approximately ten seconds before recognizing a kindred spirit. The handshake lasts three seconds. The technical conversation that follows lasts four hours.

By the end of it, they've redesigned the cascade modulation plan three times, each iteration more elegant than the last. Kael provides the theoretical framework. Vessel provides the practical engineering. Together, they're building something that no single discipline could achieve.

'The fragments need to be arranged in a specific geometric pattern,' Kael explains, sketching on a display. 'A seven-point resonance array, with the keystone at the center. When activated by a Voidborn consciousness, the array generates a standing wave in the dimensional barrier, reinforcing it at the exact frequencies where the tears tend to form.'

'I can build the mounting array,' Vessel says. 'Precision-machined, calibrated to the fragments' individual frequencies. The challenge is the power distribution. If the cascade overloads any single node, the array fails.'

'That's where our friend comes in,' Kael says, looking at you. 'You modulate the power. Your consciousness acts as a load balancer, directing energy where it's needed, compensating for fluctuations in real-time.'
'No pressure,' Vessel adds, with a flat delivery that might be humor.

choices.text: "Build the array. I'll get the keystone."
"How long until the array is ready?"

vbd_075.title: Into the Foundry
text: The Foundry's industrial sublevel is a maze of heat and metal. You lead the way, your awareness extended, reading the security infrastructure the way you once read the radiation signatures of distant stars. Guard positions, sensor arrays, patrol patterns, all mapped in your consciousness like a three-dimensional blueprint.

You guide the team through the facility's defenses with the precision of a surgeon. A psychic nudge here, redirecting a guard's attention. A telekinetic touch there, disabling a sensor without triggering the alarm. The fragments vibrate against your body, their resonance growing stronger as you approach the keystone.
Sub-level four. The main lab. And there it is.

The Resonance Engine sits in the center of a chamber that dwarfs anything you've seen in this world. A column of captured between-space, held in place by a ring of phase-dampening emitters, pulsing with a frequency that makes reality flex like a drum skin. And at its base, integrated into the emitter ring, the keystone fragment glows with a violet light that is unmistakably, heartbreakingly familiar.
It's the largest piece of you. The part that remembers what you were before the fall. The part that connects the other fragments into a whole.
And standing beside it, as if she's been expecting you, is Dr. Elara Voss.

choices.text: Confront Dr. Voss
Reach for the keystone with your power

vbd_076.title: One More Look
text: You extend your awareness through the Foundry's infrastructure, mapping every detail of Division 9's operation. The facility is bigger than you expected, deeper, more complex. Dozens of researchers, hundreds of data terminals, equipment that hums with the frequency of the between-space.

And something else. In a wing you didn't know existed, behind phase-dampened walls that nearly hide it from your awareness, a holding area. Cells. Not empty. People. Shadowkin, held in containment, their phase abilities suppressed by technology that makes your stabilizer look like a toy.

Subjects. Division 9's test subjects. The ones Pyx mentioned. The ones with numbers instead of names, locked in cells beneath a factory floor, contributing data to a project that treats their existence as raw material.

Your awareness brushes against theirs. Frightened minds, dulled by sedation and isolation, but still aware. Still hoping. Still human enough to feel fear and loneliness and the desperate wish that someone, anyone, would come for them.
The mission just got more complicated. You can't just take the keystone. You have to take the people too.

choices.text: Modify the plan. We rescue everyone.
The keystone first. Come back for the prisoners.

vbd_077.title: Last Night Human
text: You spend the last night at Sera's shop. Or what might be the last night, if the mission goes wrong, if the cascade can't be controlled, if the cosmic consciousness you're about to unleash consumes the human body it's been living in.

Sera makes rice. Real rice, not the nutrient bar substitute. She cooks it on the camp stove in the back room, the same stove where she first taught you to eat, two years ago, when holding chopsticks was a greater challenge than spanning light-years.

You eat in silence. The rain drums on the roof. The neon from the Market District filters through the window, painting the walls in colors that you've learned the names of. Red. Blue. Green. Colors that the void didn't have because the void didn't need them.

The rice is warm. Simple. Perfect. A few grains stuck to the bottom of the bowl. You eat them one at a time, savoring each one, because you are a cosmic consciousness that has learned to appreciate the miracle of a single grain of rice, and that appreciation is the most human thing about you.
'Sera,' you say.
'Yeah?'
'Thank you for finding me.'
She reaches across the table and squeezes your hand. 'Thank you for being worth finding.'

choices.text: Morning comes. Time to save the world.

vbd_078.title: Fold Alliance
text: Lira considers your request. Around her, the Fold watches, twenty-three Shadowkin whose lives have been defined by the same dimensional phenomenon you're trying to seal.

'You want us to help you raid a Division 9 facility,' Lira says. 'The same Division 9 that's been hunting us for years. The same facility where they keep the Resonance Engine that could be used to weaponize our abilities.'
'Yes.'
'And if we succeed, the dimensional barrier gets sealed. The phasing stops. Or at least becomes controllable.'
'That's the theory.'
Lira looks at her glass hand. The hand that Division 9 left in the between-space. The hand that represents everything they took from her and everything she survived.

'We're in,' she says. 'Not for you. Not for the barrier. For the children they're holding in those cells. For Subject 47 and every number that came before. For the seventeen names on that wall who phased and never came back because Division 9 pushed them past their limits.'
She extends her glass hand. 'The Fold stands with you. But when this is over, Division 9 answers for what they've done. That's not negotiable.'

choices.text: "Deal. Let's plan the assault."
"Division 9 answers. I promise."

vbd_079.title: Engine Intel
text: You share everything you know about the Resonance Engine. The column of captured between-space. The emitter ring. The keystone fragment that powers it. The entities pressing against the barrier from the other side.

Lira's face goes through several expressions, none of them pleasant. 'They're using our technology against us,' she says. 'The phase-dampening emitters, the containment field, it's all based on research they conducted on Shadowkin subjects. On us. They dissected our abilities and built a machine from the results.'

'And now that machine is the biggest threat to the dimensional barrier since the original experiment,' you add. 'The Engine is keeping the between-space portal open. If we shut it down, we remove the largest artificial weakness in the barrier.'
'But the keystone,' Lira says. 'You need it for your seal.'

'I need it. And I need to remove it from the Engine without causing the containment to fail catastrophically. If the between-space column collapses uncontrolled, it takes out everything in a three-block radius.'

Lira nods slowly. 'Then we need a controlled shutdown. And for that, we need someone who understands the Engine's systems from the inside.' She pauses. 'I know someone. A former Division 9 engineer. She builds stabilizers out of a shop in the Market District.'
'Mira,' you say.
Lira blinks. 'You know her?'
'This city is smaller than it thinks.'

choices.text: Bring Mira into the operation
Finalize the plan with the Fold

vbd_080.title: Dr. Voss
text: Dr. Voss regards you with the clinical interest of someone observing a specimen that has finally, after years of patience, walked into the collection jar.

'The Scrap Yards entity,' she says. 'We've been waiting. The keystone has been responding to your proximity for the past hour. The resonance patterns are extraordinary. You're more complete than our models predicted.'
'You know what I am,' you say.

'We know what the fragments are. We know they're pieces of something that came through the anomaly. We know they react to a consciousness signature that we've been tracking since the impact event. What we don't know is what happens when all seven pieces are reassembled.' She smiles. 'But I think you do.'
Behind you, the team holds position. Sera at the entrance, Kael with his scanner, others at their posts. The plan is in motion. You just need to keep Voss talking long enough.
'I know what happens,' you say. 'The barrier seals. The tears close. The between-space entities stay on their side. And your Engine becomes the most expensive paperweight in corporate history.'
Voss's smile doesn't waver. 'Or the Engine channels the seal's energy into a controlled portal. Permanent access to the between-space. Unlimited dimensional energy. Under our management, of course.'
'Your management destroyed lives. Imprisoned children. Created a weapon from stolen abilities.'
'Invested in a future,' Voss corrects. 'A future you're about to help us achieve, whether you intended to or not.'

choices.text: "Now," you signal the team
Reach for the keystone with everything you have

vbd_081.title: Seizing the Keystone
text: You reach. Not with hands. With everything you are.

The containment drops. The walls you've built inside your mind dissolve. For one blinding, terrifying, transcendent moment, you are what you were before the fall. Vast. Limitless. A consciousness that spans the space between thoughts and fills it with the light of a thousand dying stars.

The keystone responds to you like a limb you didn't know was missing. It tears free of the emitter ring with a sound like reality cracking, a deep, structural groan that resonates through the Foundry's foundations. The Resonance Engine screams. The column of between-space writhes, its containment failing, the entities inside it pressing against a barrier that is suddenly, catastrophically thinner.

You catch the keystone in a telekinetic grip that could crush mountains. You catch the between-space column in a psychic field that holds reality together through sheer force of will. You hold both, the fragment of yourself and the wound in the world, and you don't let go.

Alarms scream. Division 9 personnel scatter. Dr. Voss shouts orders that nobody follows because the world is coming apart at the seams and the only thing preventing total collapse is a cosmic consciousness in a borrowed body, standing at the epicenter of a dimensional crisis, holding everything together with love and terror and the stubborn refusal to let go.

choices.text: Hold on. Don't let go. Save everyone.

vbd_082.title: The Moment
text: The team moves. Through the chaos, through the failing containment, through the screaming personnel and the groaning infrastructure, your people do what you trained them to do.

Shadowkin phase through walls, reaching the holding cells, freeing the prisoners. Sera coordinates the evacuation, her voice cutting through the noise like a blade. Kael monitors the dimensional readings, calling out warnings as the barrier fluctuates. Everyone plays their part.
And you hold the world together.

It's the hardest thing you've ever done in any form of existence. Harder than spanning light-years. Harder than being compressed into flesh. Harder than learning to eat and breathe and feel and love. Because this isn't about being vast or being small. It's about being both at once. Cosmic and human. Infinite and finite. Powerful enough to seal a dimensional rift and humble enough to care about the people on the other side.

The fragments resonate in your grip. All seven now, unified, singing a frequency that aligns with the crystal beneath the city. The ancient presence stirs in the bedrock, adding its billion-year patience to your two-year determination.

The seal begins to form. Not a wall, not a barrier, but a healing. The tears in space close like wounds knitting shut. The between-space entities recede. The column of captured nothing collapses inward, sealing itself, the dimensional breach closing with a sound like a sigh.

And the power flows out of you. The vastness. The cosmic consciousness. Everything you were before the fall, pouring through the crystal, through the fragments, into the seal. Fuel for a barrier that will hold for millennia.

choices.text: Let go of the vastness. Choose humanity.

vbd_083.title: Fragment Resonance
text: With multiple fragments in your possession, the resonance between them creates something unexpected. A field. Not electromagnetic, not psychic, something else entirely. A dimensional stabilization effect that extends outward from the fragments in a sphere approximately fifty meters in radius.

Within this sphere, reality is firmer. More defined. The subtle fluctuations that characterize the Lower District, the phase events, the dimensional bleed-through, the occasional warping of physical laws - all of it stops. Inside the field, the world works the way it's supposed to.

You discover this when a Shadowkin passing through the tunnels near your camp suddenly solidifies. Their flickering outline firms up, their phase ability temporarily suppressed, and they stare at their own hands with an expression of wonder.
'What did you do?' they ask.

'I'm not sure,' you say. But you understand the implication. The fragments, assembled, stabilize the dimensional barrier locally. Seven fragments, properly arranged, could stabilize it globally. Permanently.

The seal isn't just possible. It's inevitable. The fragments want to be assembled. They want to heal the breach. They're pulling toward each other with a force that transcends physics, drawn by the same fundamental principle that draws a wound toward closure.

choices.text: Use this knowledge to plan the final assembly
Study the stabilization effect further

vbd_084.title: Dreams of the Void
text: You dream of the void every night. Not nightmares. Not anymore. The dreams have changed from the early days when every sleeping moment was a replay of the fall, the compression, the terror of becoming small. Now the dreams are different. Quieter. More contemplative.

In the dreams, you stand at the boundary between the void and the world. On one side, the vast cold dark, the infinite emptiness that was your home for longer than this planet has existed. On the other side, the warm, chaotic, beautiful mess of physical reality. Rain and neon and the smell of rice cooking on a camp stove.
And you, standing in between. Not torn. Not conflicted. Balanced. A bridge between two states of being, holding both in your consciousness without either one consuming the other.

The ancient presence speaks to you in the dreams. It says: THIS IS WHAT YOU WERE MADE FOR. Not by it. Not by the anomaly. Made by the two years of living, of learning, of becoming something that neither the void nor the world has seen before.
You wake to the sound of rain and the smell of Sera's coffee, and the balance holds.

choices.text: Face the day. The mission is close.
Tell Sera about the dreams

vbd_085.title: The Gathering
text: They come from the tunnels, from the market stalls, from the bars and back rooms and hidden spaces of the Lower District. Shadowkin and cyborgs and baseline humans and beings that defy classification, drawn together by the shared knowledge that something is coming and they'd rather face it standing up than hiding.

You stand before them in the sanctuary, the fragments arranged around you in a rough circle, their violet glow painting the cavern in colors that speak of the void between stars. Your team flanks you. Sera, practical and fierce. Kael, brilliant and exhausted. Lira, damaged and unbreakable. The others, each one carrying their own weight of fear and determination.
'In a few hours,' you say, 'we go to the Foundry. We face Division 9. We take the keystone. And we seal the barrier between this world and the void.'
You look at them. These small, mortal, impossibly brave people.

'I fell from the sky two years ago. I didn't choose this world. I didn't choose this body. I didn't choose any of you. But if I could choose, if a consciousness that once spanned the spaces between galaxies could look at every world in every reality and pick one to fight for, I would choose this one. This city. These people. You.'
The silence lasts three heartbeats. Then Sera breaks it. 'You heard the alien. Let's go save the world.'

choices.text: Move out

vbd_086.title: Reality Warping
text: The closer you get to activation, the more reality responds to your presence. Small effects at first. Water flowing upward in your vicinity. Lights brightening when you walk past. The ambient temperature rising by two degrees in whatever room you occupy.

Then larger effects. A building's structural supports rearranging themselves into a more efficient configuration as you pass through the lobby. A broken comm unit repairing itself in your pocket, its circuits realigning along optimal pathways. The rain parting above your head, forming a dry column that follows you down the street.
Reality is accommodating you. As if the world itself recognizes what you are and is making room. Adjusting. Preparing.

It terrifies the people around you. Even Sera flinches when a streetlight explodes and then un-explodes, the glass shards flying upward to reform into a perfectly intact bulb. The involuntary reality warping is getting stronger, harder to control, and the implications are clear.

The body can't contain you much longer. The cosmic consciousness is expanding, pressing against the shell, demanding to be released. The fragments are accelerating the process, each one adding resonance, adding power, pushing you closer to the moment when you'll have to choose between staying human and becoming what you were.
The clock is ticking. Measured not in hours but in heartbeats.

choices.text: It's now or never. Begin the mission.
Meditate. Hold the containment a little longer.

vbd_087.title: Sera's Gift
text: Before you leave for the Foundry, Sera hands you something. A small object wrapped in cloth, warm from being carried close to her body.
You unwrap it. Inside is a grain of rice. A single, perfect grain, preserved in a tiny capsule of clear resin.
'The first grain you ever ate,' Sera says. 'I saved it. Don't ask me why. I'm sentimental about stupid things.'

You hold it up to the light. A grain of rice. The most mundane object in the most mundane world you've ever inhabited. A fragment of organic matter so small it would be invisible from orbit, let alone from the vast distances you used to span.

And it means everything. Because this grain represents the moment you stopped being a cosmic consciousness trapped in a cage and started being a person learning to live. The moment Sera put food in front of you and said eat, and you discovered that a body has opinions, and those opinions start with hunger.

You pocket the resin capsule. Next to the fragments, next to the technology that will seal the dimensional barrier, a grain of rice in a plastic shell. The most powerful anchor you have to the world you're about to fight for.
'Thank you,' you say.
'Come home,' Sera says. 'That's all the thanks I need.'

choices.text: Head for the Foundry. Save the world.

vbd_088.title: Division 9 Raid
text: Division 9 responds to the intrusion with the organized violence of a corporation defending its most valuable asset. Neural stunners discharge in blue arcs. Phase-dampening fields snap to life, forcing nearby Shadowkin into solidity. Automated defense systems activate, deploying countermeasures designed for threats that don't follow the normal rules of physics.
But they didn't design their countermeasures for you.

You extend your awareness through the facility's electronic infrastructure. Every system, every circuit, every processor falls under your psychic influence. Security doors open for your team and close behind them. Defense turrets track targets that don't exist. Communication systems broadcast conflicting orders, turning coordination into chaos.

The facility's AI recognizes the intrusion and attempts to lock you out. For a moment, your consciousness and the artificial intelligence face each other across the facility's network, two forms of non-human awareness sizing each other up.
The AI is sophisticated. Complex. Powerful by human standards.
You were a consciousness that spanned light-years.
The contest lasts approximately four seconds.

choices.text: Clear the way to the Engine room
Free the prisoners while the defenses are down

vbd_089.title: Liberation
text: The holding cells open simultaneously, every lock disengaged by your psychic command. Fourteen people stumble out into the corridor, blinking in the harsh light, their phase-dampening collars deactivated, their suppressed abilities flooding back like blood returning to a numbed limb.
Shadowkin, every one of them. Division 9's collection, their research subjects, the living data points of Project Threshold's decades-long experiment in dimensional exploitation.

Some cry. Some stagger. One, a woman who's been in containment for four years, drops to her knees and phases through the floor, then phases back up, sobbing with the relief of someone who's been held motionless for years and can finally move.
Lira is there. She gathers them, organizes them, speaks to them in the quiet, steady voice of someone who's been exactly where they are and survived it.
'You're free,' she says. 'Not safe. Not yet. But free. And we're getting you out of here.'

The freed Shadowkin join the evacuation. Some can barely walk. Others phase through walls with the desperate speed of the newly liberated. All of them move, because staying is not an option when the walls are shaking and the Engine in the next chamber is about to be permanently shut down.
You watch them go and feel something that the void never taught you. The satisfaction of doors opening instead of closing.

choices.text: Now. The keystone. The seal. The choice.

vbd_090.title: The Threshold
text: The Engine chamber shakes as the between-space column destabilizes. The entities inside press harder, sensing the weakening containment, their hunger translated into dimensional pressure that makes the walls groan and the floor crack.

You stand at the center, the fragments orbiting your body like satellites, the keystone in your hands. The ancient presence beneath the city rises to meet you, a billion years of patience culminating in this moment, in this body, in this choice.

The seal is ready. The fragments are aligned. The crystal deep beneath the Foundry resonates with a frequency that makes the air sing. All that's needed is the catalyst. Your consciousness, expanded to its full cosmic scope, channeled through the crystal, poured into the barrier like mortar into cracks.
And then you'll be empty. Human. Mortal. Small.
Sera's voice reaches you through the chaos. 'Come home,' she said. Come home.

You close your eyes. You think about rice. About rain. About the sound of laughter in a language you learned word by word. About the weight of a body that was never yours but became yours through the slow, patient alchemy of living in it.
You think about Sera. About Kael. About Lira and Dex and Vessel and every strange, impossible person who chose to stand with a cosmic refugee because standing together is what humans do.
You open your eyes. You reach for the vastness. And you let it go.

choices.text: Seal the barrier. Choose humanity.

vbd_091.title: The City Responds
text: As the seal activates, the city above responds. Every person with phase sensitivity, every Shadowkin, every dimensional anomaly in the Lower District feels the shift. The between-space retreats. The dimensional barrier firms. The constant, subliminal pressure of the void lifts from the world like a weight being removed from tired shoulders.

In the tunnels, Shadowkin stabilize. Their flickering outlines firm up. The involuntary phasing stops. For the first time in their lives, they are fully, completely, unambiguously solid. Not cured. Changed. Stabilized. The phase ability remains, but it answers to their will now, not to their fear.

In the streets above, the atmospheric readings improve. The chemical haze thins slightly. The rain falls cleaner. It's not dramatic, not transformative, just a small improvement in a city that needs many. But it's real, and it starts here, in a chamber beneath the Foundry, where a cosmic consciousness is pouring itself into a seal that will protect this world for millennia.

The ancient presence adds its strength, a billion years of patience and power flowing alongside your sacrifice. Together, two Voidborn consciousnesses, one ancient and embedded, one young and embodied, weave a barrier that will hold against the void's hunger for longer than human civilization has existed.
The cost is real. The vastness flows out of you like heat from cooling metal.

choices.text: Complete the seal

vbd_092.title: The Between Retreats
text: The column of between-space collapses. Not explosively, not catastrophically, but like a door closing. The entities inside recede, pulled back into the void by the seal's force, their hunger denied, their reaching fingers retracted. The between-space withdraws from the world like a tide going out, leaving behind the clean, firm ground of stable reality.

The Engine goes dark. The emitters power down. The hum that has been vibrating through the Foundry's foundations for years stops, and the silence that replaces it is the most beautiful sound you've ever heard.

Dr. Voss stands amid the ruins of her life's work. Twenty-three years of research, of experimentation, of justifying atrocity in the name of progress, all of it rendered moot by a cosmic being who chose a grain of rice over a galaxy.
She doesn't speak. There's nothing to say. The door she spent her career trying to open has been sealed by someone who understands what's on the other side.
The fragments go dark, their purpose fulfilled, their energy spent. They're just crystals now. Beautiful, inert, the empty shells of something that once connected two realities.
Like you.

choices.text: Walk out of the chamber. Human.

vbd_093.title: Aftermath
text: The team extracts. Through the failing facility, past the stunned Division 9 personnel, up through the Foundry's industrial levels and out into the night air of the Lower District. The rain has stopped. The clouds have thinned. And above, through a gap in the permanent overcast, you can see stars.
You look up at them and feel nothing.

Not the cosmic recognition, the intimate awareness of each star's radiation signature, the gravitational embrace of distant suns. Just the flat, two-dimensional view of points of light on a dark background. Pretty. Remote. Someone else's neighborhood.
The vastness is gone. You are small. You are mortal. You are human in a way that you've been pretending to be for two years but never truly were until this moment.
It should hurt. The loss of something that vast, that ancient, that fundamentally you, should feel like amputation. Like death.

But Sera is standing beside you. And her hand is warm. And the stars are beautiful even without knowing their names. And somewhere in the city below, a noodle vendor is cooking rice, and the smell drifts up through the streets like a promise of something simple and perfect and worth protecting.

choices.text: Go home with Sera

vbd_094.title: Sera's Hands
text: Sera checks you over in the alley behind the Foundry. Her hands, calloused and strong and infinitely gentle, moving across your face, your arms, your hands, searching for damage that can't be seen from the outside.
'Are you...?' she starts.
'Human,' you say. 'Just human.'

She stops. Looks at you. Really looks. And then she does something she's never done before. She puts her arms around you and holds you, tight, the way you've seen humans hold each other when words aren't enough and touch is the only language that means what it needs to mean.
You hold her back. With arms that are just arms now. Ordinary. Limited. Capable of reaching exactly as far as a human arm can reach, and no further.
It's enough. It's more than enough. It's everything.

choices.text: Walk home together

vbd_095.title: The Morning After
text: You wake in the room above Sera's shop. The ceiling is stained with rust and condensation. The window lets in grey light and the sound of the Market District stirring to life. Everything is exactly as it was. Nothing has changed.
Everything has changed.

You sit up and the body responds with the familiar complaint of muscles that need stretching and joints that need warming. You don't have phase-sight anymore. You can't hear thoughts. You can't move objects with your mind. The headache is gone, but so is everything that caused it.
You're a person. Just a person. Waking up in a small room in a big city on a planet that suddenly feels much larger now that you can't perceive it from a cosmic perspective.
Sera calls from downstairs. 'Rice is ready.'

Rice. The word carries a weight that would be incomprehensible to anyone who hasn't been a cosmic consciousness learning to appreciate the miracle of cooked grain. You get up, stretch, and walk downstairs on feet that are exactly as heavy as they should be.
One grain at a time. One day at a time. One human life, lived fully, in a world you chose to protect.
The rice is warm. The rain starts again. And you eat.

choices.text: Begin your human life

vbd_096.title: Kael's Discovery
text: Kael finds you two days after the seal. He bursts into Sera's shop with a scanner reading that makes his hands shake.

'The seal is holding,' he says. 'Better than holding. It's strengthening. The barrier is regenerating at a rate that exceeds every model I built. Whatever you did down there, it didn't just close the door. It healed the wound.'

He shows you the data. Dimensional stability readings across the Lower District, climbing steadily, reaching levels that haven't been recorded since before Project Threshold. The phase events are declining. The between-space bleed-through is stopping. The world is becoming more solid, more real, more firmly itself.
'And the Shadowkin?' you ask.

'Stabilizing. Their phase abilities are still active, but the involuntary episodes have stopped. They can choose when to phase and when to be solid. It's like their condition has been cured. Not removed. Cured. Controlled.'
You smile. It's the smile of someone who's lost something vast and gained something small and discovered that the small thing is worth more.
'Thank you, Kael. For everything.'
He shrugs, but his eyes are bright. 'Thank the cosmic entity who taught itself to be human. That's a paper I'll be writing for the rest of my career.'

choices.text: Face the future. Human, but not alone.

vbd_097.title: New Identity
text: Without the cosmic consciousness, without the power, without the psychic abilities and the telekinesis and the reality-warping presence that marked you as something other, you need a new identity. Not the surface-level identity that Sera gave you when she named you. A deeper one. An answer to the question that every human carries: who am I?

You're not a cosmic consciousness anymore. You're not a Voidborn, not really, not without the void. You're a person with unusual memories and an impossible origin story and the knowledge that you once gave up everything to protect a world that doesn't know you exist.
You're a refugee. An immigrant from the most distant shore imaginable. A stranger who became a local through the slow, patient work of learning and adapting and caring.

You're Sera's family. Kael's friend. Lira's ally. You're a face in the crowd at the Market District, a customer at Bao's noodle stand, a resident of the Lower District who pays rent late and tips when they can and knows the names of their neighbors.
You're human. Not by birth. By choice. The hardest, most deliberate, most meaningful choice a cosmic consciousness has ever made.
And that's enough. It's more than enough.

choices.text: Step into your new life

vbd_098.title: The Legacy
text: In the deep tunnels, the crystal goes dormant. Its purpose fulfilled, its energy spent, it becomes what it appears to be: a beautiful, inert formation of crystalline material, remarkable only if you know what it once did. The ancient presence sleeps, satisfied, its billion-year vigil finally justified by the actions of a being it pulled from the void and trusted with the world's survival.

The cavern remains. The carvings remain. The record of what happened here, written in a language that spans dimensions, will endure long after the city above crumbles and new cities rise in its place.

And somewhere in that record, in the most recently carved section, is your story. Not the story of a cosmic consciousness or a Voidborn or a being of vast and terrible power. The story of a person. A person who fell from the sky and was caught by a scrap dealer and taught to eat rice and chose, in the end, to be small.
The story of a person who saved the world by deciding it was worth saving.
That's the legacy. Not power. Not vastness. Not the cosmic consciousness that once spanned the spaces between stars.
Just a choice. Made by a human. In a body that was never meant to be theirs but became, through love and patience and a single grain of rice, home.

choices.text: Go home

vbd_099.title: Stars
text: One evening, weeks after the seal, you climb to the roof of Sera's building and look at the stars.

The overcast has thinned since the seal stabilized the dimensional barrier. More light gets through, both sunlight during the day and starlight at night. The Lower District's residents have noticed, some with wonder, some with the automatic suspicion of people who've learned that changes usually mean new problems.

You look up and you see stars. Just stars. Points of light, distant and beautiful, following orbital mechanics that you can no longer perceive at a fundamental level. You can't feel their gravity. You can't taste their radiation. You can't trace the paths of their planets or sense the electromagnetic signatures of their cores.
They're just pretty lights in the dark.
You wait for the grief to hit. The mourning for what you lost. The ache of a cosmic consciousness reduced to a single pair of organic eyes staring at a sky it can no longer comprehend.
It doesn't come. Instead, something else arrives. Something quieter. Something that might be peace.
The stars are beautiful. You don't need to understand them to appreciate them. You don't need to span the distances between them to know they matter. You just need to look up and feel wonder.
Wonder, you realize, doesn't require vastness. It requires attention. And attention is the one cosmic ability you kept.

choices.text: Go inside. Tomorrow is another human day.

vbd_100.title: Fallen Star
text: The Lower District wakes around you. The same sounds it makes every morning: the hiss of atmospheric processors, the clatter of market stalls being assembled, the drone of patrol units making their rounds, the constant, omnipresent rain drumming on metal roofs and concrete walls.

You stand at the window of Sera's shop and watch the city come alive. Not with cosmic awareness. Not with psychic sensitivity. With two human eyes, slightly nearsighted, in a face that shows the wear of twenty years it hasn't actually lived.

Somewhere up there, past the clouds and the atmospheric processing layers and the orbital platforms, the barrier between realities holds firm. The seal you powered with the vastness you gave up is doing its job, silently, invisibly, protecting twelve million people who will never know how close they came to the void.
That's all right. They don't need to know. Heroism isn't diminished by anonymity. If anything, it's purified by it. You didn't save the world for recognition. You saved it for rice.
Sera hands you a bowl. Warm. Simple. Perfect.

You eat. And outside, the rain falls on a world that is small and loud and dirty and beautiful and yours. Not because you own it. Because you chose it. Because a consciousness that once spanned the spaces between galaxies looked at this cramped, chaotic, improbable place and said: this is worth everything I am.
You finish the rice. You wash the bowl. You step outside into the rain, into the noise, into the teeming, impossible miracle of a life lived in a body you've learned to call home.
The stars will be there tonight. You'll look up at them and feel wonder. And wonder, you've discovered, is the one thing that doesn't require vastness.
It only requires a heart that chooses to stay.

choices.text: Step into the rain. Begin again.
